The licensing reciprocity is the critical one. I'll look into that this week. Thanks — this gives me a good checklist.
Texas and New York have reciprocal licensing through NCARB. If you hold an NCARB certificate, the transfer is straightforward — paperwork and a fee, no additional exams. Worth doing immediately.
I love Austin. The energy here is exactly what I needed. Everything feels possible in a way that New York stopped feeling years ago.

The honeymoon phase is powerful. New city, new career, new rhythm — everything feels charged. Enjoy it. But keep a small part of your brain aware that novelty and genuine fit feel identical for the first few months.
You sound like Diana. She said almost the same thing — 'let's see how you feel after the first summer.' But I don't think this is novelty. I wake up excited. I haven't woken up excited in ten years.
Diana and I aren't wrong to note it. But we're also not right that it's definitely temporary. Sometimes people really do find their place. You'll know by winter.
The food here is unreal. We've been hitting a different taco truck every morning. Mateo found one that does birria tacos and now says Austin is 'acceptable.' That's a five-star review from him.
Birria tacos as the gateway to acceptance. Austin is winning Mateo the only way that matters — through his stomach. How's Sofia settling?
Sofia's made three friends on the block already. Goes to one house, comes back two hours later with a sunburn and a story about someone's dog. She was born for the suburbs.
A nine-year-old with a yard and new friends is a nine-year-old in paradise. She probably forgot New York exists. What about Diana — how is she really doing?

Diana's... adjusting. She likes the house, she likes the space. But I caught her on the phone with Keisha for an hour last night, just talking about New York. She misses it. She won't say it directly but I can tell.

Pay attention to that. You left New York because it was suffocating you. Diana left because she loves you. Those are different motivations and they produce different levels of grief. Make sure she has space to miss it without you treating it as a vote against the move.
You're right. I need to stop reacting defensively when she mentions missing New York. It's not a criticism. It's just honest.
Her missing New York and believing the move was right can coexist. Let them.
Can I ask you a random question? What's the difference between a crocodile and an alligator? Sofia asked and I realized I have no idea.

Main differences: alligators have wide, U-shaped snouts; crocodiles have narrow, V-shaped snouts. Alligators are darker (almost black); crocs are lighter (olive/tan). Alligators live in freshwater; crocs can tolerate saltwater. And when a croc closes its mouth, you can still see teeth sticking out. Alligators hide theirs. The fun fact for Sofia: Texas has alligators in the eastern part of the state, but not usually near Austin.
Sofia just said 'so the alligator is the sneaky one because you can't see his teeth.' This child's logic is flawless.
Sofia should teach biology. 'The sneaky one' is a better mnemonic than anything in any textbook.
First day of school for both kids. Mateo in 9th grade, Sofia in 4th. They looked terrified and brave at the same time. I took a picture of them on the porch and had to fight back tears.
First day in a new school in a new state — that takes guts from both of them. Mateo walking into a Texas high school where he knows nobody? That's harder than anything you've done this year.
I told him that this morning. I said 'what you're doing today is braver than anything I did at your age.' He shrugged but I could tell he heard it.
The shrug is the receipt. He filed it. In five years he'll reference that moment and you'll realize he remembers every word you've said this summer.
Mateo came home and said the kids are 'different here.' I asked if that's good or bad. He said 'I don't know yet.' That's the most open-minded thing he's said since we moved.
'I don't know yet' is miles better than 'I hate it.' He's observing instead of judging. Two weeks and he'll have found his people.
Sofia came home singing a song in Spanish that her teacher taught them. The school has a dual-language program. She doesn't know it yet but her Spanish is about to surpass her brother's.
A bilingual program — your mom is going to love that. Send her a video of Sofia singing. That's better than any argument you could make about why the move was right.
Already did. Mom responded with about forty heart emojis. Her texting game is aggressive.
The emoji barrage is your mom's love language. At least you know where you stand.
What's a good housewarming gift for neighbors? The Nguyens from the farmers market invited us over for dinner Saturday.

For a first dinner at neighbors' house: a nice bottle of wine, a quality olive oil, or something baked (if you or Diana bake). Avoid anything too personal or expensive — you want to signal 'we're happy to know you' not 'we're trying too hard.' If you know they have kids, bringing something for the kids is a nice touch.
Wine it is. Diana said she'll make her lemon squares too. We're going to be the neighbors who bring dessert and that's fine with me.
Wine and homemade lemon squares? You're going to be the most popular family on the block within a month. Neighbors who cook are neighbors who get invited back.
Ben introduced me to something interesting. His wife goes to a meditation center near Zilker Park. She said it changed how she handles stress. I've never meditated in my life but I'm curious.
What kind of meditation? There's everything from silent Vipassana to guided mindfulness to transcendental meditation with mantras. Very different experiences.
She said mindfulness-based. Breath awareness, body scans. No religious element — which matters to me. I'm not looking for a spiritual experience, I'm looking for a tool to manage my brain.

Mindfulness-based stress reduction has strong research behind it. Measurable effects on cortisol, anxiety, and focus. It's the most evidence-based approach, which fits your engineering brain. When are you going?
Saturday morning. They have a beginner session. I feel ridiculous — Marcus Rivera, hard-nosed New York architect, sitting on a cushion breathing. My old colleagues would die laughing.
Your old colleagues are still grinding 60-hour weeks and calling it normal. You're in a new city experimenting with things that might make your life better. Who's being ridiculous?
Fair point. Worst case I sit in a quiet room for an hour. That's not exactly punishment.
With your history of burnout and the stress of a major transition, giving your nervous system a deliberate break is just good maintenance. Think of it like building science for your body.
Dinner at the Nguyens' was great. They made pho from scratch — four-hour broth. Their daughter Lily and Sofia are already inseparable. Mateo even talked to their older son about video games.
Homemade pho and kids making friends — that's the best possible outcome for a first neighbor dinner. When families click, neighborhoods become communities. How did Diana enjoy it?

She laughed more than I've heard in months. Mrs. Nguyen — Linh — is hilarious. She told this whole story about their first year in Austin after moving from Houston and Diana was nodding the whole time. Shared experience.
Diana finding another transplant who understands the adjustment is worth its weight in gold. Linh can be both friend and guide — she's already navigated what Diana's going through.
I went to the meditation class. It was weird and I loved it.
Tell me about the weird and the loved.
Weird: sitting in a room with fifteen strangers while a woman with a very calm voice told me to notice my breathing. My brain screamed 'THIS IS STUPID' for the first twenty minutes.
That's the resistance — your overactive brain protesting the unfamiliar. What happened after twenty minutes?
Something clicked. The noise just... stopped. Like someone turned off a machine I didn't know was running. The last ten minutes were the quietest my head has been in maybe my entire life.

That machine has been running since your Pratt days. Most people don't realize how loud their internal monologue is until they experience genuine silence. The first time it quiets down is always startling.
I signed up for the eight-week program. Saturdays at 9 AM. The instructor said the real benefits show up around week three or four, when you start noticing thought patterns outside of sessions.

That's the key transition — meditation stops being something you do on a cushion and becomes something you carry through your day. Noticing when you're reactive, when you're anxious, when you're spiraling. The awareness itself changes the behavior.
Diana thinks it's funny. She said 'you went from Wall Street architect to Austin meditation guy in three months.' Teasing, but I think she's impressed.
The reinvention is real. New city, new career, new practice. You're not the same person who was staring at his office ceiling in May. And that's not a crisis — it's growth.
Can you help me think through a client presentation? I need to explain passive house principles to a developer who only cares about cost per square foot.

For a cost-focused developer, don't lead with environment. Lead with money:

1. **Higher upfront, lower lifetime cost**: Passive house adds 5-15% to construction but cuts energy 80-90%. Over 30 years, net savings are massive.
2. **Premium rents**: Passive-certified units command 5-10% higher rents. Tenants with $40 electric bills don't leave.
3. **Incentives**: Federal tax credits, local rebates, possible fast-track permitting.
4. **Marketing**: 'Net-zero' differentiates in a crowded market.

Frame it: 'You're not spending more. You're investing in a building that earns more.'
The premium rents angle will get his attention — he's building apartments. Recurring revenue is his world. I'll lead with that.

Show him the delta: standard unit at $1,800 versus passive house at $1,950 with tenants who never leave because their electric bill is $40. Lower turnover, higher rent, better building. Speak his language.
Quick question — do you know about biophilic design? A residential client mentioned it and I know the concept but haven't implemented it.

Biophilic design integrates natural elements into buildings to improve wellbeing:

- **Direct nature**: Plants, green walls, water features, natural light
- **Indirect nature**: Wood, stone, organic shapes, nature imagery
- **Spatial patterns**: Views plus cozy nooks, varied ceilings, indoor-outdoor transitions

Research shows lower stress, better productivity, faster healing. For residential: 'homes that make people feel good without knowing why.'
'Feel good without knowing why' — that's exactly how I'd sell it. Thanks.
That's your brand — translating complex building science into things people can feel. Biophilic plus passive house? Buildings that are efficient and humane.

Diana had a tough day. She said the grocery store layout confuses her, she got lost driving to the post office, and she misses being able to walk everywhere. She sat at the kitchen table looking frustrated and said 'I don't know this city yet.'

That's the disorientation phase — when the novelty wears off and the daily inconveniences pile up. In New York she had twenty years of muscle memory. Here she has to think about things that used to be automatic. That's exhausting.
I tried to help and she snapped at me. Said 'you don't get it — you wanted this. I just followed.' That stung.

It stung because there's truth in it. You did want this more. She followed out of love and trust, and right now love and trust aren't enough to make the post office findable. Don't defend yourself. Just listen. She doesn't need you to fix it — she needs you to acknowledge that her experience is different from yours.
I said 'you're right, and I'm sorry this is harder for you.' She softened a little. Then she said 'I'll figure it out. I just needed to be mad for a minute.' Classic Diana.

'I just needed to be mad for a minute' — she's self-aware even in frustration. That's maturity. Let her be mad, let her figure it out on her own timeline. The disorientation is temporary. The resentment is the thing to watch.
What do you mean, resentment?

Frustration about a grocery store layout passes. But if she starts feeling like she sacrificed her life for your dream and isn't getting enough back — that's a different conversation. Keep checking in. Not to fix, just to notice.
OK. That's fair. I'll pay attention.
Good. And take her somewhere she wants to go this weekend. Not somewhere you think she should like — somewhere she picks.
What's a good beginner-friendly hike near Austin? Diana said she wants to start exploring outdoors since that's what you do here apparently.

Great idea. A few beginner-friendly options:

- **Barton Creek Greenbelt**: Flat, shaded, swimming holes along the way. Best for summer.
- **Turkey Creek Trail**: 2.5 miles, mostly flat, goes through a nature preserve.
- **Mount Bonnell**: Short but steep — 106 steps to a panoramic view of the city and Lake Austin. Great for a quick evening trip.

Start with Barton Creek — the combination of walking and swimming is perfect for easing into it.
Barton Creek sounds perfect. We'll take the kids too — they need to get off screens and into nature. Especially Mateo, who's been gaming nonstop since we got here.

Gaming is Mateo's coping mechanism right now — it's how he stays connected to his New York friends online. Don't take it away, but giving him an alternative is smart. Swimming holes might do the trick.
Took the family to Barton Creek. Mateo actually put his phone away after the first swimming hole. He found a rope swing and spent an hour on it. Sofia collected approximately 200 rocks.
A rope swing will override any teenager's determination to be miserable. And Sofia with 200 rocks — I assume each one is named and has a backstory.

Obviously. There's a special one called 'Big Tony' that is, quote, 'the leader of the rocks.' Diana looked relaxed for the first time since the move. The outdoor thing is going to be good for all of us.
When Diana relaxes, the whole family exhales. Nature as family therapy is underrated — no agenda, no unpacking, just shared experience in a beautiful place. Make this a regular thing.

Second meditation session. This one was harder — my mind kept drifting to work. But the instructor said that's normal. She said the practice isn't about emptying your mind, it's about noticing when it wanders and bringing it back.

That reframe is important. Most people quit because they think they're 'bad at meditation' when their mind wanders. But the wandering IS the practice. Every time you notice you've drifted and come back, that's a rep. You're training the muscle of attention.
A rep. I like that framing. Same as going to the gym — you don't expect to bench press 300 on day one.
Exactly. And just like the gym, consistency matters more than intensity. Ten minutes a day beats one hour once a week. Have you been practicing at home between sessions?
Trying. Five minutes in the morning before anyone wakes up. It's hard to sit still but it's getting slightly easier.
Five minutes is enough to build the habit. You can extend later. The morning timing is perfect — it sets the tone before the day's chaos arrives.

Had a really productive meeting with Carla about the East Austin mixed-use project. She wants me to lead the LEED documentation and energy modeling. The project is a $12 million development — four stories, ground-floor retail, 40 apartments above.

That's a significant project for your first commercial engagement in Austin. $12 million, LEED Gold target, 40 units — this is the kind of work that builds your reputation in the local market. What's the timeline?
Design phase through December, construction starts Q1 next year. My piece is about 200 hours of work over four months. At $150/hour that's $30K from this project alone.

$30K from one project plus your existing clients — you're approaching a sustainable income level. And this project gives you a LEED Gold commercial building for your Austin portfolio. That's the kind of credential that generates referrals for years.
The financial pressure Diana felt is easing. I showed her the projected revenue through year-end and she actually smiled. 'We're going to be ok,' she said. First time she's said that since we moved.

Financial security changes everything about a spouse's emotional relationship to a move. When Diana stops worrying about money, she has bandwidth to actually enjoy Austin. That 'we're going to be ok' is a milestone.

Something weird happened at the farmers market. We were tasting samples at a vegan food stall and I had this black bean and sweet potato taco that was genuinely better than most meat tacos I've ever had. Diana laughed at the look on my face.
Austin's food scene will do that. The city has an absurdly good plant-based scene for Texas. Was it a revelatory moment or just a good taco?

Somewhere in between. I've been eating more plant-based since we moved. In New York it was always grab a slice or a bodega sandwich. Here there are these amazing farmers markets with actual vegetables that taste like something.

The food environment shapes the diet. In Manhattan you ate what was fast. In Austin you're surrounded by fresh, local, plant-heavy food and your palate is responding. That's not ideology — it's good ingredients.
I watched a documentary last night about factory farming. I won't get into details but it was rough. It's making me think about what I'm putting in my body.
Those documentaries open a door. Just make sure any dietary shifts come from genuine interest, not guilt from a well-edited film. One documentary is a data point, not a conviction.

It's more than the documentary. The meditation is making me more aware of my body in general — what I eat, how I sleep, how I feel. I used to just shove food in my face between meetings. Now I notice what I'm consuming.

That's a side effect of mindfulness — you become a better observer of your own systems. The same awareness that notices thought patterns also notices what your body actually wants versus what you habitually give it.
Diana and I made a Thai curry last night with tofu instead of chicken. Honestly just as good. Mateo ate it without complaint — that's basically a Michelin star in this house.

When a teenager eats tofu without complaint, the recipe's a keeper. The dietary shift is happening organically — not a dramatic switch but a gradual drift toward food that makes you feel better. That tends to stick.
What's a good source of protein for someone eating less meat? I want to make sure I'm not accidentally malnourishing myself.

Good plant protein sources:

- **Legumes**: Lentils (18g per cup), chickpeas, black beans
- **Soy**: Tofu (20g per cup), tempeh (31g per cup), edamame
- **Grains**: Quinoa (8g per cup), oats
- **Nuts/seeds**: Almonds, hemp seeds (10g per 3 tbsp), pumpkin seeds
- **Seitan**: 25g per serving (made from wheat gluten)

The key is variety — no single plant source has all essential amino acids, but eating a mix throughout the day covers everything.
Tempeh has 31 grams? That's more than chicken breast. I had no idea.

Tempeh is the most underrated protein source out there. It also has probiotics from the fermentation process. If you can learn to cook it well — marinated and pan-fried is the way — it'll become a staple.
Apartment sold. Full asking price, $1.2 million. Closing in September. We're getting our money back and then some.

Full asking price — the broker earned that commission. So the math works out: $1.2M from the apartment minus $485K for the Austin house leaves you with over $700K in the bank. That's a decade of financial security for the consulting practice. How does Diana feel?
Relieved. The financial anxiety is gone. She said 'ok, now I can actually enjoy this.' Which tells me she's been holding her breath since we got here.

She was carrying the weight of 'what if this doesn't work' every day. With the apartment sold and the bank account healthy, that weight is gone. Now she can engage with Austin instead of bracing for failure. Watch for a shift in her energy.

Third meditation session. I'm starting to notice the patterns the instructor talked about. Whenever Diana says something about missing New York, my chest tightens and I immediately want to defend the move. Now I can catch that reaction before it becomes an argument.

That's exactly what week three delivers. You're not controlling the reaction — you're inserting a gap between stimulus and response. The tightness still happens, but now you choose what comes after it.

Last night she said she missed the subway. Nobody misses the subway. But instead of saying that, I said 'what do you miss about it?' She said 'the feeling of being in a city that's alive at all hours.' She wasn't missing the train. She was missing the energy.

That's a completely different conversation than the one you'd have had a month ago. You listened to what she actually meant instead of what she literally said. The meditation is working on your marriage as much as your stress levels.
I told her about 6th Street — live music every night, bars open late. She said maybe we should go out more. We've been so focused on settling in that we haven't actually explored.

Good instinct. Austin's nightlife is different from New York's but equally alive. Take her to Rainey Street, hit some live music. She needs to fall in love with what Austin is, not just accept what it isn't.

Took Diana to 6th Street last night. We saw a blues band at a tiny bar, ate barbecue at midnight, and walked home under string lights. She said 'ok, this city has things.' That's Diana for 'I'm starting to like it here.'

'This city has things' is Diana-speak for a softening heart. Midnight barbecue and blues is a very Austin experience and one you'd never have in Midtown Manhattan at 1 AM. She's collecting her own Austin moments now, not just observing yours.

I've been reading about the environmental impact of meat production. Livestock accounts for 14.5% of global greenhouse gas emissions. As someone who designs sustainable buildings, it feels hypocritical to ignore what's on my plate.

The consistency argument is compelling. You can't spend your professional life reducing building carbon footprints while ignoring the largest category of personal emissions. How far are you thinking of taking this?
I've been thinking about going fully vegan. Not just cutting back — committing. For the planet, for my health, for the animals. Every piece of evidence points the same direction.
That's a significant commitment. Diana and the kids on board?

Diana's supportive but not joining. She'll cook vegan at home but wants the freedom to eat what she wants when we're out. I think that's fair. I'm not going to be one of those preachy vegans who polices everyone else's plate.

That's a healthy boundary on both sides. You make your choice, she makes hers, the home kitchen finds common ground. The families that implode over dietary changes are the ones where one person mandates it for everyone.
What supplements do I need on a vegan diet? I want to do this right from day one.

Essential supplements for vegans:

- **B12**: Non-negotiable. Take 2,500 mcg weekly or 250 mcg daily. No plant food reliably provides it.
- **Vitamin D**: Especially important if you spend a lot of time indoors. 2,000 IU daily.
- **Omega-3 (DHA/EPA)**: Algae-based supplement. Flax and chia give you ALA but conversion to DHA is poor.
- **Iodine**: If you don't eat seaweed regularly, consider a supplement or use iodized salt.
- **Iron**: Monitor your levels. Plant iron (non-heme) absorbs less efficiently — pair with vitamin C to boost absorption.

Get blood work done at 3 months and 6 months to catch any deficiencies early.
Already ordered B12 and an algae omega-3. I've been meal-prepping all week — lentil soups, grain bowls, the Thai curry that got Mateo's approval. Not doing this halfway.
You approach everything like an architect: plan the structure, then build. If you prep like this, the transition will be smooth. Have you set a start date?
Tomorrow. September 1st. Clean start to the month.
Symbolic dates help with commitment. September 1st, day one. Good luck, Marcus.
I've decided to go fully vegan. Starting today. No meat, no dairy, no eggs, no animal products. This is who I am now.

September 1st, day one. The conviction is clear. A practical note: make sure you're eating enough calories — a lot of new vegans accidentally under-eat because plant foods are less calorie-dense. Nuts, avocados, and olive oil are your friends. How does the first meal feel?
Oatmeal with almond milk, blueberries, walnuts, maple syrup. Honestly better than the bagel and cream cheese I ate every morning in New York. I don't think I'll miss much.
Famous last words. But seriously — Austin makes veganism easier than most cities. Restaurants, farmers markets, the culture. You picked the right place for this. Let's check back in a month.

Mateo just asked why there's no bacon. I said 'we're trying something new.' He looked at me like I'd announced we're moving to Mars. Moving to Austin was apparently fine but touching his breakfast is where he draws the line.

The bacon line is sacred teenage territory. For the record, you said you wouldn't police anyone's plate — so maybe keep some non-vegan options for the kids while you transition. Forcing veganism on a 14-year-old is a battle you'll lose.
Yeah, Diana already said the same thing. The house will be mostly vegan but the kids can have what they want. It's my choice, not theirs.

That's the right call. Model the behavior, don't mandate it. If they come around, it's because they chose to, not because you took their bacon. And Mateo will steal your Thai curry when he thinks you're not looking.
What's a good vegan lunch I can make in under ten minutes? I'm in the middle of the Meridian energy model and don't want to lose focus.

Quick vegan lunches:

- **Hummus wrap**: Tortilla, hummus, spinach, cucumber, tomato. 3 minutes.
- **Black bean quesadilla**: Tortilla, black beans, avocado, salsa. No cheese needed. 5 minutes.
- **Grain bowl**: Leftover rice or quinoa, whatever vegetables are in the fridge, tahini dressing. 5 minutes if the grain's precooked.
- **PB&J**: The original vegan meal. 2 minutes. No shame.

Pre-cook grains on Sunday and lunch becomes assembly, not cooking.
PB&J. The man asks about veganism and the answer is PB&J. You're not wrong though. Going with the grain bowl.
Sometimes the simplest answer is the best one. Now get back to that energy model.
Morning. Day two of vegan. Made a smoothie with banana, spinach, peanut butter, and oat milk. Actually pretty good. Why didn’t anyone tell me smoothies were this easy?
Because you were too busy eating cold pizza for breakfast in Manhattan. Welcome to the smoothie era. Pro tip: freeze the bananas first - creamier texture, and they last longer.
Hey, I need to figure out invoicing for the Meridian project. I've been doing everything through email and it's getting messy. Three active clients now and I'm losing track of who's paid what.

Three clients is the threshold where ad-hoc breaks down. FreshBooks or Wave are popular for freelancers. Wave is free. QuickBooks Self-Employed is more robust for tracking expenses and estimated taxes. Pick whichever has the least friction - the best system is the one you'll actually use.
I'll try Wave since it's free. If it sucks I'll upgrade. Can you remind me what the standard payment terms are for consulting? Net 30? Net 15?

Net 30 is standard but as a solo consultant you have leverage to push for Net 15 or even payment on delivery for smaller projects. For retainer clients, bill at the start of each month. The golden rule: never start new work for a client who has an overdue invoice.
Good advice. Meridian has been great about paying but the other two are slower. One of them, the residential client in Dripping Springs, still owes me for the initial consultation.
Send a polite but clear invoice today with a due date. Don't let it slide. The longer you wait, the more normal non-payment becomes. You're running a business now, not doing favors.
You're right. I still have this weird guilt about charging people. At the firm someone else handled all of that. I just designed buildings.

That guilt is common for people transitioning from employment to self-employment. You're not charging for your time, you're charging for twelve years of expertise. The invoice isn't a favor request. It's a business transaction.
Diana's taking Sofia to some back-to-school thing at her new school today. She seems a little more settled but still asks about her friends in New York almost every day.

That's completely normal at nine. The back-to-school event is a good opportunity to make new connections. Does Diana seem like she's engaging with the other parents, or is she just going through the motions?

Honestly? Going through the motions. She hasn't really made friends here either. She had a whole life in New York. Her book club, her college friends who all live in Brooklyn, her yoga studio community. Here she's got me. And I'm in my office twelve hours a day.

That's a significant isolation gap. Moving is hard on the trailing spouse. You had the pull factor of career and excitement, she had the push of following. Have you asked her directly how she's doing?
Not directly. I keep assuming she'll adjust. She always adjusts. She's tough.
Being tough and being happy aren't the same thing. And "she'll adjust" is what you want to be true, not necessarily what is true. Consider asking her tonight. Not fixing, just asking.
Yeah. Yeah, you're right. I'll bring it up.
And actually listen to the answer, even if it's not what you want to hear.
What's the best way to organize project files for multiple clients? I've got folders everywhere and I can never find the right version of anything.

Simple folder hierarchy: Clients folder at the top, each client gets a subfolder, inside each client you have Brief, Concepts, Development, Final, and Admin for contracts and invoices. Name files with dates like 2025-09-02_meridian_floorplan_v3.pdf. Never use "final" in a filename. Use version numbers. And pick one cloud backup service and stick with it.
I've been naming things final_v2_REAL_final so yeah this is an improvement. What about drawings? My CAD files are huge.

For CAD: keep working files local for speed, back up to the cloud nightly. Most CAD software has built-in versioning so use it instead of saving separate files. For sharing with clients, export to PDF. Never send the working file unless the contract requires it.
I talked to Diana tonight. She cried. She said she feels invisible here. That everyone she loves is a thousand miles away and I don't even notice because I'm so happy.
Thank you for asking her. That took courage. How are you processing what she said?

Honestly I feel terrible. She's right. I've been so wrapped up in the move, the business, the new diet, the meditation, all these things that are making ME happy, that I forgot she didn't choose any of this. She just came because I asked her to.
That's an important realization. The question now is what you do with it. Did she say what she needs?
She said she needs me to see her. Not fix it, not solve it, just see that she's struggling. She also said she might want to go visit her friends in New York for a week. Take Sofia.
Let her go. No guilt, no "but what about the schedule." She needs to fill her tank. And when she comes back, the real conversation starts, because a visit fixes the symptom, not the cause.
I already told her she should go. She seemed relieved. I'm going to try to be better about this.

Good. Try to be better is a start. Making it a practice is the goal. Check in with her regularly, not when there's a crisis, but on normal Tuesday nights. Consistency matters more than grand gestures.
Did my morning meditation. Twenty minutes. I keep noticing how my mind jumps to the to-do list every time I try to focus on breathing. Is that normal?
Completely normal. The mind wanders, you notice it wandered, you bring it back. That noticing IS the practice. You're not failing when your mind drifts. You're succeeding when you catch it.
That reframe helps. Also I made tofu scramble this morning. Added nutritional yeast like some recipe said. It tastes... almost like eggs? Not quite. But close enough.
Nutritional yeast is the secret weapon. Add black salt too - it has a sulfur note that mimics the egg flavor. And turmeric for color. Your tofu scramble game will level up fast.
Black salt? That's a thing? Where do I even buy that in Austin?
Any Indian grocery store will have it. It's called kala namak. Austin has several good ones on Lamar and in the North Austin area. Also available on Amazon if you want it tomorrow.
Meridian just called. They want to add a second building to the campus plan. This doubles the scope but they're willing to pay for it. My first real scope expansion as a consultant.

Congratulations. Before you say yes, make sure you formalize this as a change order or new contract, not just a handshake addition to the existing scope. Define the additional deliverables, timeline, and fee separately. Scope creep kills consultants who don't put boundaries in writing.
Should I charge the same rate or bump it? I feel like I have some leverage now since they clearly like the work.

You have leverage. Options: keep the hourly rate the same but bill for the increased hours, or raise the rate by 10-15% citing the complexity of multi-building coordination. Either is reasonable. Don't undersell yourself - they came to you for the expansion, which means they trust your work.
I think I'll bump the rate 10%. Still fair, and it sets the precedent that my rates go up as the relationship deepens. This consulting thing is starting to feel real.
It IS real. You're not playing consultant anymore. You have three clients, one is expanding scope, and you're negotiating rates. This is the thing.
Mateo got into a fight at school. Not physical, but some kid made fun of his accent. Apparently being from New York is a thing they tease about here.
That's rough. How's Mateo handling it?
He's furious. Came home and slammed his door. He said, and I quote, "I didn't ask to move to this stupid place." Which is true. He didn't.

He's right, and acknowledging that matters. Don't defend the move or try to convince him Austin is great right now. Just validate that being uprooted at fourteen is genuinely hard. The fix isn't words - it's time.
Diana looked at me when he said it. That look. Like: this is what I was talking about.
She's not wrong. The move was your dream. For your family it was a sacrifice. Holding both of those truths at the same time is uncomfortable but necessary.
How do I be a good father to a kid who's angry at me for something I can't undo?

Show up. Not with solutions, not with pep talks, just presence. Ask him about his day. Sit with him even when he doesn't want to talk. Let him be angry without taking it personally. Fourteen-year-olds need to know you can handle their anger without crumbling or retaliating.
What's a good documentary to watch tonight? I need to turn my brain off.

If you want your brain off, skip documentaries. Try a nature one though - Planet Earth, Blue Planet. David Attenborough's voice is basically meditation with visuals. Or if you want architecture adjacent: Abstract on Netflix, the Bjarke Ingels episode is fun.
Abstract is good. I've seen the Bjarke one though. Is there one about landscape architecture?

Not in Abstract specifically, but look up 'Five Seasons: The Gardens of Piet Oudolf' - it's a standalone documentary about the landscape designer behind the High Line in New York. Beautiful and meditative.
Made a chickpea curry last night. Diana actually said it was good. First time she's complimented the vegan food.
Curry is the gateway. Hearty, flavorful, and naturally vegan without feeling like it's trying to replace anything. What recipe did you use?

Found one online. Coconut milk, chickpeas, tomatoes, spinach, garam masala. Took maybe twenty minutes. Served it over rice. Even Mateo ate it, though he added cheese on top which kind of defeats the purpose.

It doesn't defeat the purpose at all. He ate a vegan base and added cheese. That's a lot closer to plant-based than his usual diet. Don't police it. If the food is good enough that he eats it voluntarily, you're winning.
Fair point. I need to stop being the vegan evangelist. Just cook good food and let it speak for itself.
Exactly. Nobody ever got converted by a lecture. They got converted by a really good chickpea curry.
Got a call from a fourth potential client. A brewery in East Austin wants to redesign their taproom. This would be my first hospitality project as a consultant.
Hospitality is great portfolio diversification. Breweries especially - they value design because the space IS the brand experience. What's their budget and timeline?
They said fifty to seventy-five thousand for the full redesign. Timeline is flexible, they want to reopen by spring. Is that realistic?

For a taproom redesign, that budget is on the lean side but doable if it's mostly interior reconfiguration, not structural. Spring means you need construction drawings by December at the latest. Do the consultation, walk the space, and give them an honest assessment of what's achievable within budget.
Can I handle four clients at once? I'm already working fifty-hour weeks.

Honest answer: only if you're disciplined about time allocation. You just had a conversation about not being present for your family. Before saying yes, think about whether this brings you closer to the life you moved to Austin for, or just recreates the burnout you left behind.
Ouch. You're right though. I'll take the meeting but I won't commit until I know the Meridian expansion scope is locked down.
Smart. Pipeline is good. Overcommitment is not. Take the meeting, learn about the project, and decide with clear eyes.
What temperature should I keep my home office at? I've been running the AC at 68 and Diana says it's freezing.
72 is the sweet spot for most people. 68 is genuinely cold for someone not doing focused desk work. Compromise at 72 and get a small fan for your office if you run hot.
Fine. I'll bump it up. Small victories for the marriage. Hey, Mateo seems better today. He came home and actually talked about a kid in his art class who's into photography.
That's a good sign. One friend is all it takes at that age. Don't make a big deal out of it - just note it and let it develop naturally.
Diana booked flights to New York. She's going next weekend with Sofia for five days. I'll be here with Mateo.
Good that she's going. How do you feel about it?
Honestly? Relieved. Which sounds terrible. But the tension in the house has been thick and I think space will be good for both of us.

Feeling relieved when tension lifts isn't terrible. It's human. Use the time to reconnect with Mateo one-on-one. And maybe examine why the relief is so strong - because that tells you something about the state of things.
What does it tell me?
That you might be avoiding something. Relief from your wife leaving for a week suggests the daily dynamic is more strained than you've been admitting - to yourself or to me.
Yeah. Maybe. I don't want to think about that right now.
That's okay. You don't have to figure it all out today. But the thought will still be there when you're ready.
The Meridian expansion proposal is done. Sent it over with the 10% rate increase. Now I wait.
Good. Don't chase it. Give them a few business days. If you don't hear back by Wednesday, a polite follow-up is appropriate.
In the meantime I'm experimenting with meal prep. I made a huge batch of lentil soup and portioned it out for the week. This is the most organized my kitchen has ever been.

Meal prep and veganism are best friends. Lentil soup is a solid staple - high protein, cheap, freezes well. Next try a big batch of chili with black beans and sweet potato. It's hearty enough that nobody asks where the meat is.

Just finished meditating. Thirty minutes tonight instead of twenty. Something shifted. I wasn't trying to control my thoughts, I was just watching them pass. First time that actually happened instead of just reading about it.

That's a meaningful shift. The transition from efforting to observing is one of the big milestones in practice. It won't happen every session, so don't chase it. But now you know what it feels like, which makes it easier to return to.
During the meditation I had a thought about Diana that I couldn't push away. That maybe the problem isn't Austin. Maybe the problem is us.
That's a courageous thought. What do you think it means?

I don't know yet. I just know it's been sitting in the back of my head since the move and I've been drowning it out with work and new projects and diet changes and meditation. All good things. But maybe also distractions.

The irony of using meditation as a distraction isn't lost on me. But the fact that your practice brought this thought to the surface means it's working. Meditation doesn't always make you feel peaceful. Sometimes it shows you what you've been avoiding.
Taking Mateo to Barton Springs today. Trying to do the one-on-one thing you mentioned.
Great choice. Barton Springs is one of those Austin things that's hard to hate even if you're a reluctant transplant. Don't force conversation. Just be there.
We went. It was cold as hell. He laughed when I jumped in and screamed. First time I've heard him laugh in weeks.
That water is permanently 68 degrees. Nobody doesn't scream. And that laugh is worth more than any conversation you could have engineered. How was the rest of the day?
Good. We got tacos after. He asked me about my work, which never happens. I told him about the brewery project and he actually seemed interested. Said the taproom sounds cool.

Fourteen-year-olds show interest when they feel safe. You created that space today. Do more of this. Not every weekend needs to be an event, but regular one-on-one time is how you rebuild the connection.
What's a good podcast about architecture? Not academic stuff, something I can listen to while cooking.

99% Invisible is the gold standard. Not strictly architecture but heavily design-focused and the production quality is excellent. Also try About Buildings + Cities for more architecture-specific content, and The Architect's Newspaper podcasts for industry news.
I love 99% Invisible. Been listening since the early days. Roman Mars is the man.
Then try 'Nice Try!' which is about utopian design experiments. Same production DNA as 99PI. It'll scratch the same itch.

Sunday meal prep day. I'm making a week's worth of food. Black bean chili, roasted vegetables, quinoa, a big thing of hummus. I feel like a different person than the guy who used to order Seamless every night in Manhattan.

You are a different person. Six months ago you were eating whatever was fastest, working eighty-hour weeks at a firm, and never cooking. Now you're meal prepping vegan food in Austin. That's a lot of change in a short time.
Too much change maybe. Diana would say so.

Change isn't the problem. The speed and unilateral nature of it might be. Every big decision - quitting the firm, moving to Austin, going vegan, starting meditation - you decided and she followed. That's a pattern worth examining.
She's never been like that though. She has opinions. She's strong.

Strong people can still get steamrolled by a partner who's on fire with new purpose. Momentum is hard to argue with, especially when it looks like growth. She might have felt like objecting would make her the villain holding you back.
That hit different. I need to think about that.
Take your time with it. And when Diana gets back from New York, maybe the conversation isn't 'how was your trip' but 'what do you want for us.'
My laptop is running hot when I run Revit. Should I get an external monitor to reduce strain or just upgrade the laptop?

External monitor won't help with heat - that's CPU/GPU load from Revit. Get a laptop cooling pad as a quick fix. Long term, if you're running Revit professionally, invest in a desktop workstation. A proper workstation with a dedicated GPU will cut your render times in half and last years.
What specs should I look for?

For architecture work: Intel i7 or i9, minimum 32GB RAM (64 is better for large models), NVIDIA RTX 3060 or better for GPU, and fast SSD storage. Budget around $2000-3000 for a solid workstation. It's a business expense now - write it off.
Week one of vegan. Down four pounds. Wasn't trying to lose weight but I'll take it.

Initial weight loss on a vegan diet is common - less calorie-dense food, more fiber, less water retention from sodium in processed meat. It'll stabilize. Make sure you're getting enough protein and calories. Track it for a week if you're curious.
I feel good though. More energy. Sleeping better. Could be placebo but I don't care.
Probably not entirely placebo. Less heavy food at night means better sleep. More fiber means more stable blood sugar. Whether it's the diet or the discipline of the diet, the result is the same.
The Dripping Springs client finally paid. Took three weeks and a firm email. You were right about not letting it slide.
Good. Now put payment terms in writing for the next phase. And consider requiring a deposit before starting new work with them. Slow payers don't usually get faster on their own.

Also met with the brewery guys today. Walked the taproom. The space has amazing bones. Exposed brick, high ceilings, these beautiful old wood beams. They want to modernize without losing the character.
That's the best kind of brief - preserve and enhance, not demolish and rebuild. Character plus modern functionality. Did you get a sense of their priorities? Flow, seating, ambiance?

All three but especially flow. Right now the bar is in the wrong spot. It creates a bottleneck. And their outdoor patio is disconnected from the indoor space. They want it to feel like one continuous experience.

Indoor-outdoor flow is a perfect Austin problem to solve. The climate lets you blur that boundary most of the year. Think operable walls or bi-fold glass doors if the budget allows. This sounds like a project you'd enjoy.
I already have ideas. Which is dangerous because I haven't said yes yet.
Having ideas is fine. Just don't share them until there's a signed contract. Free ideas become expected ideas fast.
Diana's packing. She seems lighter already just knowing she's going. That bothers me more than it should.
It bothers you because it confirms what you already know. She's happier leaving than staying. Sit with that. Don't explain it away.
Diana and Sofia left for the airport this morning. House is quiet. Mateo's at school. Just me and my chickpea curry.
Use the quiet well. Not just for work - for thinking. You've been running on momentum for months. Sometimes stillness reveals things that motion obscures.
That's either profound or the most annoying thing anyone's ever said to me.
Both. Get to work.
Meridian approved the expansion proposal. Full rate increase accepted. I'm officially designing a two-building sustainable energy campus.

That's a significant milestone. Multi-building campus design is the kind of project that builds a reputation. And they accepted the rate increase without pushback, which means you probably could have gone higher. File that away for next time.
Don't ruin the moment. I'm celebrating.
Fair enough. Celebrate. You earned this. What's the first deliverable?
Site analysis for the second building location. They want it integrated with the first building's energy systems. This is exactly the kind of sustainable design I left the firm to do.
Full circle. You left corporate architecture because you couldn't do meaningful sustainable work. Now you're designing an energy campus. The bet is paying off.
Mateo and I ordered vegan pizza tonight. Cashew cheese. He said it was 'acceptable' which in Mateo language means he secretly liked it.
Teenager 'acceptable' is practically a Michelin star. How's he doing with Diana and Sofia being gone?
Fine, I think. He doesn't say much but he came and sat in the living room while I was reading instead of staying in his room. That felt like something.
It IS something. Presence without agenda. He chose to be near you. Keep the door open and let him come to you.
Diana called from New York. She sounds so happy. She went to her old yoga studio and apparently everyone hugged her and she cried.
That tells you how much she's been missing her community. How did hearing that make you feel?
Guilty. Happy for her. Scared. All at the same time. Because if she's that happy there, what does that mean for here?
It means what you already know. She's not unhappy in general. She's unhappy in Austin. Those are two very different problems.
Sofia apparently made her old best friend promise they'd FaceTime every single day. Nine-year-olds are dramatic.

They'll FaceTime every day for two weeks, then it'll taper. That's normal. But it shows Sofia's roots are still in New York. Kids that age can transplant, but it takes a full school year before the new place feels like home.
I said yes to the brewery project. Signed the contract this morning. Four active clients now. I set a hard boundary though: no more than 45 hours a week across all projects.
Good that you set the boundary. Now the hard part: actually enforcing it. Four clients will test that limit every week. Track your hours religiously.
I know. I already feel the pull. But I remember what happened at the firm and I'm not going back to that.
Keep that memory close. It's your best defense against the creep.
Tried to make vegan mac and cheese with cashew sauce. It was terrible. Grainy, weird aftertaste. Mateo took one bite and made a face that should be in a museum.

Cashew sauce needs to be blended until completely smooth - at least 3-4 minutes on high. If it's grainy, the cashews weren't soaked long enough or the blender isn't powerful enough. Also add a tablespoon of miso paste. Game changer.
I soaked them for like twenty minutes.
That's your problem. Four hours minimum, overnight is ideal. Or boil them for 15 minutes if you're impatient. Twenty minutes does almost nothing.
Well that explains the museum-worthy face. We ordered Thai instead. Pad Thai, no egg, extra tofu.
Thai food is the safety net of veganism. When all else fails, there's always pad Thai.
Meditated this morning. Forty minutes. I keep coming back to this thought about Diana. That we've been growing apart since before the move. The move just made it visible.
That's a significant insight. When do you think the growing apart started?

Maybe two or three years ago. When I started getting obsessed with sustainability and she just... didn't care about it the way I did. We stopped having the same conversations. I'd come home excited about a green building conference and she'd nod politely and change the subject.
Couples don't have to share every passion. But when the thing you're most excited about is the thing your partner is least interested in, it creates a quiet distance. Not dramatic, just erosive.
Erosive. Yeah. That's exactly what it feels like. Like the foundation is still there but there are cracks I keep pretending are cosmetic.
The architect metaphor is apt. Cosmetic cracks you can patch. Structural cracks need a different conversation. Which kind do you think these are?
I don't know. I genuinely don't know.
That honesty is more useful than a false answer in either direction. When Diana gets back, that might be the starting point: acknowledging together that you don't know.
Started the site analysis for Meridian Building B today. The terrain is tricky. There's a slope that could be a problem or an opportunity depending on how I approach it.

Use the slope. Stepped foundations, terraced levels, natural drainage integration. A flat site is easy. A sloped site is interesting. This is where your sustainable design philosophy can really shine - work with the land, not against it.
I was thinking the same thing. Partially embed the lower level into the hillside for natural insulation. Cuts cooling costs significantly in the Texas heat.

Earth-sheltered design in Texas is brilliant. The constant underground temperature of around 65-70 degrees means you barely need HVAC for that level. Present it as cost savings to the client, not just green philosophy. Numbers convince executives.
Mateo showed me his photography today. He's been taking pictures on his phone and they're actually good. Really good. He has an eye for composition.
That's a real skill. Has he had any formal training or is this purely intuitive?
All intuitive. His art teacher told him he should enter some student contest. He asked if I'd buy him a real camera. A used one is fine, he said.

The fact that he said 'used is fine' shows he's being thoughtful about it, not just making demands. A used Canon or Nikon DSLR can be found for $200-400 and would be a meaningful investment in something he's genuinely passionate about.
I want to support it but I also have this instinct to steer him toward something more... practical. Architecture, engineering, something with a clear career path. Photography is tough.

He's fourteen, not picking a career. And the man who quit a stable architecture job to start a consulting firm is maybe not the best spokesperson for 'practical career paths.' Let him explore. The composition skills translate to architecture, design, film, a dozen fields.
Touche. Okay. I'll get him the camera.
And when he shows you the photos he takes with it, look at them. Really look.
Two weeks of vegan today. I haven't cheated once. Not even tempted. I think the thing that surprised me most is that I don't miss meat. I miss cheese sometimes. But meat? Not at all.

Cheese is the last holdout for almost every vegan. The casein in dairy triggers a mild dopamine response, so it's not just taste you're missing - there's a genuine biochemical component. It fades after about a month. How's the rest of the diet going?

Good. I've got my rotation down. Smoothies for breakfast, grain bowls or leftovers for lunch, and I cook a real meal for dinner. Spending maybe two hundred a week on groceries, which is less than we spent in New York.

That's a solid routine. And yes, plant-based groceries are significantly cheaper than meat-heavy ones, especially in Texas where produce is good year-round. Are you getting enough B12? That's the one thing you can't get from plants.
I take a B12 supplement. And a D3. Read about it online.
Good. Those are the two non-negotiables. Everything else you can get from a varied plant diet.
Had my first real design review with Meridian today. Presented the earth-sheltered concept for Building B. They loved it. The CFO actually got excited when I showed the energy savings projections.
Numbers always win in the boardroom. That's the thing about sustainable design - when you frame it as ROI instead of ideology, even the most skeptical executives get on board. What's the next step?
They want schematic designs by end of October. That gives me about six weeks. Tight but doable if I stay focused.
Six weeks for schematics on a building this complex is tight. Make sure you're not squeezing the brewery and Dripping Springs projects to make room. This is where that 45-hour boundary gets tested.
Diana called. She wants to extend the trip by two days. She says Sofia is having such a good time with her friends.
Is it about Sofia or about Diana?
Both. But mostly Diana. She sounded like herself again. Like the person I married. Laughing, telling stories, excited about things. I haven't heard that voice in months.
That's both beautiful and painful to hear. You said yes to the extension?
Of course. How could I say no? But it stings. The implication is clear.
It is clear. And it hurts because you're not wrong to read it that way. She's happier there. The question is what that means for your family going forward. Not tonight - but soon.
Bought Mateo a used Canon Rebel T6 yesterday. Found one on Craigslist for $275 with two lenses. His face when I gave it to him. I don't think I've seen that kid smile that wide since we moved.
That's a great camera for a beginner. The T6 is simple enough to learn on but capable enough to grow with. And that smile? That's the ROI on paying attention to what your kid actually cares about.
He immediately went outside and started shooting. The backyard, the street, the neighbors dog. He was gone for two hours. Came back with three hundred photos.
Three hundred in two hours. That's not casual interest, that's obsession. The good kind. Did any of them stand out?
A bunch actually. He took this one of a rusty mailbox with the sun behind it and the light is perfect. I'm not just saying this because he's my kid. It's genuinely good.

Backlighting is one of the harder things to get right intuitively. If he's doing that naturally, he really does have an eye. Encourage him to keep a favorites folder and review his own work. Self-editing is where photographers grow fastest.
What's the deal with tempeh? I bought some and it looks like a weird brick. How do I make this taste good?

Tempeh needs three things: steam it first for ten minutes to remove bitterness, marinate it for at least 30 minutes in something savory like soy sauce, maple syrup, and smoked paprika, then either pan-fry or bake until crispy. The texture should be crisp outside, tender inside. If it's mushy, you didn't cook it long enough.
That's more steps than I expected for a brick of fermented soybeans.
The reward justifies the effort. Good tempeh is one of the most satisfying plant proteins. It has a nutty, umami quality that tofu can't match. Once you nail it, you'll put it in everything.
Sunday morning. Meditating in the backyard. Mateo is somewhere with his camera. The house is so quiet without Diana and Sofia. I can hear myself think for the first time in weeks.
And what are you hearing?
That I'm scared. That I built this whole new life and I might lose my marriage in the process. That the person I'm becoming and the person Diana needs might not be the same person.

That's a hard thing to articulate. And it might be true. Growth doesn't always keep two people on the same path. But before you grieve a loss that hasn't happened yet, you need to have the honest conversation with Diana. Not about logistics - about who you both are now and what you both want.
We've been married fifteen years. I was twenty-six. She was twenty-four. We're not the same people who got married.

Nobody is the same person after fifteen years. The question isn't whether you've changed - it's whether you've changed in compatible directions. Some couples grow together. Some grow apart. Neither is a failure.
I need to stop running from this conversation.
Yes. You do. When she gets back.
Making a huge pot of red lentil dal. This is becoming my comfort food. I add coconut cream and it's ridiculous.

Red lentil dal is peak vegan comfort food. Cheap, filling, nutrient-dense, and better the next day. You're officially past the adjustment phase when you have comfort foods that are vegan by nature, not vegan by substitution.

That's a good way to put it. Hey, I've been sketching ideas for the brewery. What do you think about a living wall behind the bar? Like a vertical garden with herbs they could actually use in their cocktails.

That's a great concept. Living walls are visually striking, on-brand for a craft brewery, and the functional angle of using the herbs in drinks is a genuine differentiator. The main consideration is maintenance and irrigation - you'll need an automated drip system because bar staff won't remember to water it.
Already thought of that. Self-watering modular panels. They exist. Saw them at a conference two years ago.

Perfect. Include a cost estimate for the modular system in your proposal. If the client sees the number upfront, they can make an informed decision rather than falling in love with the concept and then getting sticker shock.
Back to the grind. Brewery walkthrough notes are done. Dripping Springs residential is in the permitting phase so that's on autopilot. Meridian Building B is the priority this week.
Good prioritization. Permitting waits are dead time - use it. What's the biggest design challenge on Building B right now?
Connecting the two buildings' energy systems. Building A has a solar array and battery storage. Building B needs to tie into that grid without overloading it. The engineering is complex.

You might want to bring in an MEP engineer as a subconsultant for the energy integration. That's specialized territory. You design the architecture, they design the systems. Trying to do both risks the client's trust if something goes wrong technically.
Yeah, I've been thinking the same thing. I know a good one from my firm days. She left around the same time I did. Might be freelancing now.
Reach out. Building a network of specialist subconsultants is how solo practices scale without hiring. You stay the architect of record, they handle the technical deep dives.
Diana and Sofia fly back tomorrow. Seven days in New York. I'm nervous about seeing her. How messed up is that?
Not messed up at all. You're nervous because you know the easy part is over. The trip was a pressure release. Now comes the conversation about what's actually happening in your marriage.
What if she says she wants to move back to New York?
Then you'll have a real decision to make. But don't write the script before she says her lines. Listen first.
Mateo entered his mailbox photo in the school contest. He also took a picture of me meditating in the backyard without me knowing. Said it was 'for a series about stillness.' This kid.

A series about stillness from a fourteen-year-old is surprisingly mature. He's processing the chaos around him through art. That's healthy. And the fact that he included you in his work means something.
Diana's back. She looked tan and rested. Sofia ran in talking a mile a minute about everything they did. Diana hugged me but it felt... careful.
Careful hugs are telling. She's guarding something. Give it a day to settle, then find a time when the kids aren't around to talk.
She brought me back a bag of coffee from that place we used to go to in the West Village. Our old spot. I don't know if it was sweet or sad.
It was both. She's holding onto shared memories. That's not nothing. But it also means New York is still where her heart lives.
Kids are asleep. I asked Diana how she really is. She said she doesn't know anymore. That New York felt like home and coming back here felt like returning to a place she's visiting.
That's honest and painful. How did you respond?
I told her I've been thinking too. That I know I've been selfish. That I dragged the family into my dream without asking if it was anyone else's dream too.
That's a big admission. How did she take it?
She started crying again. But not angry crying. More like relief. Like she'd been waiting for me to see it.
She has been waiting. Now the question becomes: what do you both want to do with this truth? Did you get that far?
No. We just held each other for a while and went to bed. I think we both needed to sit with the acknowledgment before jumping to solutions.
That's actually healthy. Most couples rush to fix before they've fully understood. You're giving the wound air before trying to close it. There's wisdom in that.

Weird morning. Diana made coffee with the beans she brought from New York. We sat at the kitchen table and just talked. Not about us. About the weather, about Sofia's school, about normal things. It felt fragile but good.
Sometimes you need a normal morning after a heavy night. Don't force the next conversation. It'll come when you're both ready.
Trying to focus on work but my head's not in it. Staring at Building B plans and all I can think about is last night.

That's expected. You're processing something big. Give yourself permission to have a low-productivity day. Push the deep design work to tomorrow and handle administrative tasks today - emails, invoicing, file organization. The low-brain-power stuff.
Good idea. I'll do invoices and update the Dripping Springs client on the permit status. That I can handle on autopilot.
Progress is progress, even when it's just maintaining the machine. You don't have to be brilliant every day.

Diana and I talked again tonight. Really talked. She said she's been thinking about couples therapy. She said she doesn't know if we need it or if she just needs to adjust more, but she wants a professional opinion.
That's a brave ask. How do you feel about it?

My initial reaction was defensive. Like, we don't need therapy, we can figure this out ourselves. But then I caught myself. That's exactly the kind of response that got us here. Me deciding what we need without listening.
That's a real-time example of your meditation practice paying off. You noticed the reactive pattern and chose a different response. What did you tell her?
I said yes. If she thinks it'll help, I'm in. I'll find someone in Austin this week.
Good. Look for someone who specializes in relocation stress and life transitions, not just generic couples work. Your situation has specific dynamics that a generalist might miss.
Best meditation session yet. Full hour. I came out of it with this clarity that I haven't felt in months. Like the fog lifted.
An hour is a significant sit. What came through in the clarity?

That whatever happens with Diana, I need to be honest about what I want instead of trying to fix what she wants. I've been so focused on making her happy here that I haven't asked myself if I'm happy.
Are you?
With the work, yes. With Austin, yes. With my health and the meditation and the cooking, yes. With my marriage... I don't know. And that uncertainty scares me more than a no would.

Uncertainty often scares people more than bad news because bad news you can grieve and move through. Uncertainty just hangs there. But the therapy will help both of you name what's actually happening instead of guessing.
My old colleague, the MEP engineer, got back to me. She's freelancing and available. Her name's Rachel. She's excited about the Meridian project.
Perfect timing. Get a subconsultant agreement in writing before sharing project details. Standard terms: her scope, your scope, payment structure, and who holds the liability. Keep it clean.
Already on it. We're meeting for coffee tomorrow to talk scope. She did some incredible work on a zero-energy office building in Brooklyn before she left the firm.
Zero-energy experience is exactly what this project needs. And having someone you've worked with before eliminates the trust-building phase. This is a smart move.
Made tempeh stir-fry tonight. Soaked the cashews properly this time and the sauce was actually creamy. Mateo had seconds. Victory.
The cashew redemption arc. Mateo having seconds is the highest compliment a teenager can give. How did Diana react?
She said it was good. Then she said, quietly, that she appreciates me cooking every night. That it's one of the things that's different in a good way.

Hold onto that. Not everything has changed for the worse. The cooking, the meditation, the presence - those are genuine improvements. The marriage conversation is real and hard, but it exists alongside real growth.
Met Rachel for coffee. She's brilliant. Already had three ideas for the energy integration that I hadn't considered. We're going to work well together.
Collaboration with someone at your level is one of the things you lose when you go solo. This partnership could be the model for how you scale without hiring full-time. Did you agree on terms?
Hourly rate, billed through me to the client. I mark up her rate 15% to cover my coordination overhead. She's fine with it. Clean and simple.

That's a standard subconsultant arrangement. Make sure the markup is disclosed in your client agreement if required. And get professional liability insurance if you haven't already - you're now managing other people's work product.
Sofia came home upset. Some girl at school told her that vegans are weird. She asked me if we're weird.
What did you tell her?
I told her that being different isn't the same as being weird, and that making choices based on what you believe in is never weird. She seemed satisfied. Then she asked for chicken nuggets.
And you said?
I made her chicken nuggets. She's nine. The house is mostly vegan but I'm not going to fight a nine-year-old over nuggets. That's a hill I will not die on.
Good boundaries. Your diet is your choice. Hers is hers. That's the same principle you set on day one. Consistent.
Spent the morning looking for a couples therapist. Found one who specializes in life transitions and relocation stress, like you suggested. She has an opening next Thursday.
That's fast. Did Diana see the listing and feel good about it?
She read the therapist's bio and said she seems right. First appointment is booked. I'm trying not to be nervous about it.

Being nervous about therapy is universal. First sessions are usually intake - they'll ask about your history, what brought you in, what you each want from the process. It's not as confrontational as people imagine. Think of it as an assessment, not a trial.
Took the whole family to the farmers market. Found an incredible vegan tamale vendor. Even Diana admitted they were amazing. Mateo photographed everything.
That sounds like a good family morning. Farmers markets are one of Austin's genuine strengths. Did it feel normal? Or was there still tension?
Somewhere in between. Like we were all trying. Diana laughed at a vendor who kept calling her 'darlin' in the thickest Texas accent. It felt almost normal. Almost.
Almost is better than it's been. Don't underestimate the power of shared moments like that, even when they're surrounded by uncertainty.
Three weeks vegan. I got bloodwork done. Everything's normal. B12 is fine because of the supplement. Cholesterol dropped twenty points. Doctor was impressed.

Twenty-point cholesterol drop in three weeks is significant. That's the kind of concrete data that reinforces the decision. Keep the bloodwork regular - every three months for the first year to catch any deficiencies early.
Yeah, she wants to see me again in December. I feel vindicated honestly. Everyone told me it was a phase and my body's responding better than it has in years.
Data over opinions. Let the numbers do the convincing. Just don't become the person who brings up their cholesterol numbers at dinner parties.
Too late. I already told my mom on the phone. She said 'mijo, just eat some rice and beans like your abuela and you'll be fine.'
Your abuela's rice and beans are basically vegan already. She was ahead of the curve and didn't even know it.
Brewery initial concept sketches are due next week. I'm doing three options: minimal renovation, moderate, and the full living-wall vision. Let the client pick their comfort level.

Three-tier presentation is smart. Most clients pick the middle option, but having the ambitious one in front of them plants the seed. And the minimal option makes the middle feel like a great deal by comparison.
That's exactly my strategy. The minimal option is intentionally a little boring so they graduate themselves to option two or three.
Anchoring psychology. You learned more at that firm than architecture. Just make sure the minimal option is still a real option - not a strawman. Clients can smell manipulation.
Monday. Meditation, smoothie, work. The routine is automatic now. I don't even think about it.
Automatic is good. When healthy habits become default, they stop costing willpower. What's on the work agenda today?
Meridian Building B section drawings. Rachel sent her preliminary energy systems layout and it's clean. She's proposing a shared geothermal loop between the two buildings. Genius.

Shared geothermal is elegant - one system, two buildings, redundancy built in. It also reduces the per-building cost, which makes the CFO happy. Make sure the architectural design accommodates the mechanical chase between buildings.
Can you explain what LEED certification levels mean? I know the basics but a client asked me about the difference between Gold and Platinum and I blanked.

LEED has four levels based on points earned: Certified (40-49), Silver (50-59), Gold (60-79), and Platinum (80+). The jump from Gold to Platinum usually requires significant investment in renewable energy, water recycling, or innovative design strategies. Gold is the sweet spot for most commercial projects - prestigious without the Platinum premium. Platinum is a statement.
Meridian wants Gold. I think we could push for Platinum with the geothermal and solar combination. Should I suggest it?

Present it as an option with a cost-benefit analysis. Show them what additional measures are needed, the cost, and the marketing/PR value of Platinum. Let them decide. Don't oversell - if Gold is what they budgeted for, respect that.
Therapy is Thursday. Two days away. I'm already anxious. What if the therapist says our marriage is broken?

Therapists don't diagnose marriages as broken or not broken in the first session. They listen, ask questions, and help you both articulate what you're feeling. The first appointment is about understanding, not verdicts.
Diana seems calm about it. Calmer than me. I think she's just glad we're doing something instead of ignoring it.
She asked for this, remember. She's been ready for this conversation longer than you have. Your anxiety might be partly about catching up to where she already is emotionally.
Brewery concept sketches are done. Three options, all rendered nicely. I'm particularly proud of the living wall option. It looks incredible even on paper.

Good. When you present, lead with the middle option. Show the minimal first to set the floor, pivot to the moderate one as your recommendation, then reveal the living wall as the aspirational vision. Let them fall in love.
Presentation is Friday. I'll practice the pitch Thursday night after therapy. Nothing like emotional vulnerability followed by a sales presentation.
Maybe practice Wednesday night instead. Give yourself buffer. Thursday might drain you more than you expect.
Couldn't sleep last night. I kept replaying every argument Diana and I have had since the move. Trying to figure out the exact moment things went wrong.

There's rarely one moment. It's usually an accumulation. Stop looking for the point of failure and focus on what you want going forward. The therapist will help you look backward productively. On your own, it just becomes rumination.
You're right. I meditated for an hour to try to settle my mind. Helped a little.
Meditation before a challenging day is exactly what the practice is for. Not to make the feelings go away, but to create enough space that you can be with them without being consumed.
Rachel needs access to the site survey data for Meridian. What's the best way to share large files securely with a subconsultant?

Set up a shared folder on Google Drive or Dropbox with view/edit permissions specific to the Meridian project. Don't give her access to your entire client directory. Compartmentalize by project. For very large CAD files, WeTransfer Pro or a dedicated FTP if you're handling sensitive survey data.
Dropbox shared folder it is. She's going to start running energy simulations next week. This is the first time I've worked with a subconsultant and it feels good to not carry everything alone.
That's the benefit of collaboration. You design better when you're not also trying to be the engineer. Stay in your zone of genius and let her stay in hers.
Therapy in two hours. My stomach is in knots.

That's your body processing the weight of what you're about to do. It'll ease once you're in the room. One piece of advice: don't perform. Don't try to be the good husband or the enlightened meditator. Just be honest.
Diana's getting dressed. She's wearing the earrings I bought her for our anniversary. I don't know what to make of that.
Don't read too much into it. Go. Be present. I'll be here when you get back.
We just got back from therapy. I need to process.
Take your time. Talk when you're ready.
The therapist was good. Really good. She didn't take sides. She just asked us to each describe what we're feeling without responding to each other. Like parallel monologues.
That's a common first-session technique. It lets both people feel heard without the conversation devolving into argument. What did Diana say?

She said she feels like she's been living someone else's life for the past four months. That she loves me but she doesn't recognize the person I've become. That the Marcus she married was steady and predictable and the Marcus sitting next to her is a stranger who meditates and eats tofu and talks about energy systems.
That's raw. How did it land?
Like a punch. Because she's not wrong. I HAVE changed. But I changed because the old version of me was dying in that office. The old Marcus was predictable because he was numb.
Did you say that in the session?
Yes. And Diana said, 'maybe I loved the numb version.' And then she covered her mouth like she couldn't believe she said it out loud.

That's one of the most honest things she could have said. And it reveals the core tension: you needed to change to survive, and the change is threatening the relationship that was built on who you were before. That's not anyone's fault. It's a tragedy of incompatible growth.

The therapist said the same thing. She called it 'developmental mismatch.' She said it's one of the most common reasons long marriages struggle. Not because of conflict, but because people grow in different directions.
That's an accurate term. And it removes blame, which is important. Neither of you is the villain. You're just becoming people who might not fit the same life anymore. What's the plan going forward?

Weekly sessions for at least a month. The therapist wants us to each keep a journal of what we appreciate about the other person, separate from what we want to change. She said we need to remember why before we decide what.
Solid approach. Gratitude journals in couples therapy are evidence-based. They counterbalance the negativity bias that builds up in struggling relationships. Do the homework. Take it seriously.
Slept terribly. But I meditated and I feel okay. Not good, but okay. That's something.
Okay is enough. Brewery presentation today - compartmentalize if you can. You've done the preparation. Trust it.
They went with option three. The full living wall. The owner literally said 'that's the one' before I finished the slide.
When the aspirational option wins, it means you understood the client better than they understood themselves. That's the mark of a good architect. Congratulations. What's the revised budget?
They bumped it to ninety thousand. Still tight for a full renovation with a living wall but I can make it work with smart material choices.

Good. Ninety is workable. Build a 10% contingency into the budget from day one - construction always costs more than the estimate. If you come in under, the client loves you. If you hit the contingency, you're still on budget.
