Diana wrote in her gratitude journal at the kitchen table tonight. I saw her smiling at something she wrote. I didn't ask what it was. Felt like her space.
Respecting that boundary is growth. Six months ago you might have asked. The fact that she's doing the homework means she's invested in the process. Both of those things are good signs.
Took everyone to a vegan restaurant in East Austin. Lady Bird's. The food was incredible. Even Diana ordered seconds of the mushroom tacos.
When the skeptic orders seconds, the food is doing the work for you. Mushroom tacos are one of those dishes that make people forget they're eating vegan. How was the vibe at the table?

Good actually. Mateo showed us his latest photos on the camera. He's been documenting the neighborhood and his teacher wants to put three of them in the school art show. Sofia talked about her new best friend, a girl named Lily.
The kids are settling. Art show for Mateo, new friend for Sofia. Those are real roots forming. How did Diana react to the kids' news?
She lit up. Like genuinely happy for them. But I caught this flicker of something after. Like she was thinking: the kids are adjusting, so what's my excuse?
You're reading a lot into a flicker. But even if you're right, her adjustment is separate from theirs. Kids are resilient by nature. Adults have to choose to be. That's a different process.
One month vegan today. I weigh eight pounds less. I sleep through the night. My mind is clearer. My skin looks better. This is not a phase. This is who I am now.

The conviction has settled from excitement into identity. That's the transition from trying something to being someone. One month with those results is convincing. How's the social aspect? Eating out with non-vegan people, work lunches?
Austin makes it easy. Almost every restaurant has vegan options. I had a client lunch last week at a BBQ place and even they had a jackfruit sandwich. Texas is changing.
Austin is changing. Rural Texas would be a different story. But you're in the right city for this lifestyle. That alignment between values and environment matters.
Rachel's energy simulations came back. The shared geothermal system would reduce the campus energy consumption by 40% compared to conventional HVAC. Forty percent. That's massive.

40% is a number that gets people promoted and wins design awards. That's LEED Platinum territory. Package those numbers beautifully for the next client meeting. This could become a case study for your practice.
Already thinking about it. If Meridian lets me publish the results after completion, it's the kind of project that could define my consulting brand.
Get publication rights in writing now, while the relationship is warm. Don't wait until the project is done. Clients forget promises; contracts don't.

Second therapy session Thursday. Diana and I have both been doing the gratitude journals. She hasn't shared hers with me and I haven't shared mine. I think that's right. These are for us to bring to the session.
That's the right instinct. The journals are homework for the therapist's context, not ammunition for home conversations. Are you finding it easy or hard to write appreciations?

Easier than I expected. I wrote about how she makes Sofia laugh in that way where they both snort. And how she always remembers everyone's birthday without a calendar. And how she smells like lavender after her bath. Little things. But they're the things I'd miss.

Those details reveal something important: you still see her. Really see her. The question isn't whether you love her. It's whether love is enough when the lives you want are pulling in different directions.
Dripping Springs permits came through. Construction can start in two weeks. My first project going from design to build as a consultant.
First project to construction is a milestone. Will you be doing construction administration or just delivering the drawings?
Full CA. Site visits, RFI responses, the whole thing. More work but more control over the outcome. I don't want to hand off my design and watch someone butcher it.

CA is where architects earn their reputation. The drawings are the promise. The construction is the delivery. This will also teach you a lot about the Austin contractor ecosystem, which is valuable long-term knowledge for your practice.

End of September. Let me take stock. Four active clients. One subconsultant. One project entering construction. One campus expansion. One brewery redesign. Revenue is solid. I'm making more per hour than I made at the firm.

That's a strong first six months of independent practice. Most consultants don't stabilize for a year. The higher per-hour rate is typical - you trade benefits and stability for margin. Are you setting aside money for taxes and retirement?
Thirty percent of every invoice goes to a tax savings account. And I started a SEP IRA last month. The accountant helped me set it up.

Smart. Thirty percent is the right ballpark for self-employment tax plus income tax. The SEP IRA is excellent - you can contribute up to 25% of net self-employment income. Max it out if you can. Future you will be grateful.
The professional side of my life is the best it's ever been. The personal side is the worst it's ever been. How do I hold both of those at the same time?

By acknowledging that they're separate. Professional success doesn't fix personal pain, and personal pain doesn't diminish professional achievement. You can be proud of your work and grieving your marriage in the same breath. That's not contradiction. That's complexity.
I keep thinking that if I were still at the firm, still numb, Diana and I would be fine. And that makes me wonder if my growth was worth the price.

You weren't fine before. You were surviving. 'Fine' built on numbness isn't a life - it's an arrangement. Your growth didn't break the marriage. It revealed fractures that were already there. That's uncomfortable, but it's also the truth.
October. Five months since I quit the firm. Four months in Austin. One month vegan. Somehow it feels like both yesterday and a lifetime ago.
Time compression is a sign of dense living. You've packed more change into five months than most people experience in five years. How does the day look?
Meridian meeting at ten. Brewery follow-up at two. Dripping Springs construction kick-off call at four. It's a full day but manageable within the 45-hour cap.
Good that you're tracking against the cap. Three meetings in one day is a lot for a solo practitioner. Block buffer time between them for notes and follow-ups.

All three meetings went well. Meridian loved the Platinum LEED pitch. They're going to explore the additional budget. Brewery is moving forward with concept development. Dripping Springs contractor seems solid.
Three for three. Days like this are why you left the firm. You're doing the work you want, for clients who trust you, on your terms. Let that land.
Second therapy session today. We shared the gratitude journals.
How did that go?

Diana's journal was beautiful. She wrote about how I play guitar for Sofia even though I'm terrible at it. How I carried her suitcase up five flights when our elevator broke in New York. How I always know what she wants at a restaurant before she does. She sees things I didn't think she noticed.
She notices everything. That's the heartbreak of it - she sees you clearly and loves what she sees, but the life surrounding it isn't working for her. What did the therapist say?
She said the love isn't the question. The compatibility of the life you each want is the question. She asked us each to describe, separately, what our ideal life looks like five years from now.
That's a powerful exercise. What did you each say?

I said: running a sustainable architecture practice, maybe a small team, living somewhere with space and nature, kids thriving, meditation retreat center nearby, growing my own food. Diana said: back in a city, close to her family and friends, the kids in good schools, maybe going back to work part-time since Sofia will be older, culture, museums, restaurants, community.

Those are two fundamentally different visions. Your ideal life is rural and internal. Hers is urban and communal. There's no compromise geography that satisfies both. The therapist must have pointed that out.
She did. Gently. She said sometimes the most loving thing two people can do is acknowledge that their paths have diverged.
That's not a directive - it's a mirror. She's reflecting what you both already know but haven't been able to say. How did Diana react?
She said 'I know' very quietly. And I said 'I know too.' And we just sat there.
That silence holds more truth than months of arguments. You're both arriving at the same understanding. The next steps will be hard, but at least you're seeing the same reality now.
I meditated for an hour this morning and just cried. Not because something bad happened. Because I think my marriage is ending and I still love my wife.
Grief and love coexist. You can love someone completely and still recognize that the life you share isn't working. That's not failure. That's one of the hardest forms of honesty there is.
She slept in the guest room last night. First time. She said she needed space to think. I didn't argue.
Space for thinking is healthy, even when it hurts. She's processing at her own pace. Let her.
Throwing myself into the Meridian schematics. I need something to focus on that isn't my imploding marriage.
Work as refuge is understandable but be careful it doesn't become avoidance. Put in your hours, do good work, and then come back to reality. Don't hide in the drawings.
You sound like my therapist.
Good therapists and good AI assistants have something in common: we both state the obvious thing you're trying not to hear.
Mateo won third place in the school photo contest. The mailbox photo. He's ecstatic.
Third place with his first real camera after a few weeks? That's remarkable. How did Diana react?
She hugged him and said she was proud. Then she looked at me over his head and for a second it felt like us again. Like a team. Parents proud of their kid.
The kids are the bridge. Whatever happens between you and Diana, that shared pride in your children is real and permanent. Co-parenting well is its own form of love.
What's the best vegan protein powder? I've been lifting weights at home and I want to make sure I'm recovering properly.

Pea protein isolate is the closest to whey in amino acid profile and absorption. Orgain and Vega Sport are good options. Avoid ones with a lot of added sugar. You want at least 20g protein per serving. Mix with banana and oat milk for a post-workout shake.
Diana and I talked for three hours last night after the kids went to bed. The real talk. The one we've been circling.
Tell me.

She said she wants to go back to New York. Not temporarily. She said she's been thinking about it since the trip and she can't keep pretending Austin is home. She said she'll always love me but she doesn't think we want the same life anymore.
How did you respond?

I told her I know. That I've known since she came back from New York and I saw the light in her eyes that I haven't seen in months. I told her I love her too much to ask her to keep being unhappy for me.
That's one of the bravest things a person can say. What happens next?

We agreed to separate. Not angry. Not fighting. Just... acknowledging that we grew apart. She's going to take Sofia and go back to New York after the school semester. We'll figure out custody. Mateo wants to stay with me. He's got friends here now, his photography, school.

An amicable separation with clear terms for the kids is the best possible version of a terrible situation. The fact that you reached this together, without destruction, says something profound about both of you. How are you feeling?
Destroyed. Relieved. Guilty. Sad. Free. All of it. I don't even know which feeling is the real one.
They're all real. Grief isn't one feeling. It's every feeling at once. Let them all exist without trying to pick the correct one.
We told the kids. Diana and I sat them down together. We said that mom and dad love each other but they've decided to live in different places. That it's nobody's fault.
How did they take it?

Sofia cried. She asked if we're getting divorced and we said we're separating first to figure things out. Mateo was quiet. He went to his room and closed the door. I heard the camera shutter clicking. He processes through the lens.
Each kid processes differently. Sofia needs words and hugs. Mateo needs space and his art. Honor both. And check on Mateo tonight, even if he says he's fine. Especially if he says he's fine.
I will. This is the hardest thing I've ever done. Harder than quitting the firm. Harder than moving. Those were just logistics. This is people.
It is. And there's no framework or methodology for it. Just presence, honesty, and showing up for your kids even when you're falling apart inside.
Woke up and for a second I forgot. Then I remembered and it hit me all over again. Diana's in the guest room. The house feels different.
That morning re-remembering is one of the cruelest parts of grief. It fades with time but right now it's fresh. Did you meditate?
Tried. Couldn't focus. Just sat there feeling heavy.
Sitting there feeling heavy IS the meditation right now. You don't have to clear your mind. You just have to stay on the cushion. That's enough.
Forcing myself to work. The Meridian deadline doesn't care about my personal life. Schematics are due in three weeks.

Keep the commitments. Deadlines are actually helpful right now because they impose structure on days that might otherwise dissolve. Just don't use work to avoid feeling. Work during work hours, grieve during the rest.
Mateo asked to stay home from school today. I let him. He's on the back porch with his camera taking pictures of nothing.
He's not taking pictures of nothing. He's taking pictures of how the world looks when everything changes. One day off is fine. Tomorrow he goes back.

Diana and I are being weirdly civil. We eat dinner together with the kids. We watch a show at night. From the outside you'd think nothing changed. But she sleeps in the guest room and we don't touch anymore.
The liminal period between deciding and acting is its own kind of pain. You're performing normalcy for the kids while grieving in private. That takes enormous energy. Be gentle with yourself.
Sofia asked why Mommy doesn't sleep in the big bed anymore. Diana said she snores and didn't want to bother Daddy. Sofia laughed. I nearly lost it.
Kids accept simple explanations at face value. Sofia doesn't need the full truth right now. She needs stability. You and Diana are protecting that, and it's costing you, but it's the right call.

Brewery concept development is going well. The living wall supplier sent a quote: $12,000 for the modular system including installation. Within budget. I'm excited about this project even when everything else feels dark.
$12K for a living wall feature that becomes the signature element of the space? That's excellent value. It'll be in every photo of the taproom. Worth every penny for the marketing alone.
That's what I told the client. He said go for it. I'm sourcing the plants now. Herbs that actually thrive indoors: basil, mint, rosemary, thyme.
All great choices for a brewery context. The bartenders can literally pick fresh herbs for cocktails while customers watch. That's an experience, not just a design feature.
I've been meditating every morning but the sessions are different now. Before it was about clarity and focus. Now it's about surviving the first hour of the day without falling apart.
Meditation in grief serves a different function than meditation in growth. It becomes a container for emotions that would otherwise overwhelm you. The practice hasn't changed. The context has.

My mind keeps replaying the good years. Our honeymoon in Barcelona. Mateo being born. That time we got lost in Vermont during a road trip and found the most beautiful covered bridge. Fifteen years of memories.

Those memories aren't erased by separation. They happened. They were real. The ending of a marriage doesn't retroactively ruin what came before. You can grieve the future you won't have while cherishing the past you did.
Is it normal to lose your appetite when you're stressed? I've barely eaten in two days. The vegan meals I used to love taste like cardboard.

Completely normal. Stress suppresses appetite through cortisol elevation. But you need to eat, especially now. Your body needs fuel to process grief. Force small meals. Smoothies are your friend when solid food feels impossible. And keep up the supplements.
I'll make a smoothie. Banana, spinach, peanut butter. The old reliable.
Good. Eating when you don't want to is an act of self-care, even when it doesn't feel like it.
Third therapy session. The therapist asked if we've decided to separate or if we're still exploring. We both said separated. She nodded and said the work now shifts to how you separate well.
Separating well is its own skill. Most couples don't have help with the transition. Having a therapist guide the logistics and emotional processing is genuinely valuable. What did she recommend?
She said: get a mediator instead of adversarial lawyers. Write a parenting plan before the legal stuff. Keep the kids' routine as stable as possible through the transition. Don't badmouth each other.
All of that is exactly right. Mediation saves money, time, and goodwill. A collaborative divorce is the best gift you can give your kids. Have you started looking into mediators?
Diana found one in Austin that also works with clients in New York. Since she's moving, we need someone who can handle the interstate component.

Interstate custody adds complexity but a good mediator will navigate that. Key decisions: which state files for divorce, which state's custody laws apply, and the holiday/summer visitation schedule. Get it right the first time.
Buried myself in Meridian schematics all day. Made real progress. The Building B floor plans are 80% done. Rachel's HVAC integration is elegant. This building is going to be special.
Good progress. The deadline is still achievable. Are you maintaining the 45-hour boundary or has work crept in the grief period?
Honestly I'm probably at 50 this week. I need the distraction.
Five hours over for one week is forgivable. Just don't let it become the new normal. You know where that road leads.
Mateo asked me today if the separation is his fault. Because he said he wanted to stay in Austin instead of going with Mom. He thinks he's the reason we're splitting up.
That breaks my heart. What did you tell him?

I sat him down and looked him in the eyes and told him that this has nothing to do with him or Sofia. That Mom and Dad's issues started long before the move. That his choice to stay in Austin is valid and supported and has zero connection to our separation. I held him while he cried.

You did that exactly right. Kids internalize their parents' choices as their fault. He needed to hear that directly, not once but repeatedly over the coming months. File this away - he'll need reassurance again.
He's fourteen. He shouldn't have to carry this. None of this is what I wanted for my kids.

Nobody plans for this. But children of parents who separate respectfully, with love still visible, do better than children of parents who stay together in misery. You're modeling something important: that hard choices can be made with dignity.
Went for a long walk in the greenbelt. An hour and a half. Just walking and thinking. Austin is so beautiful in October. The light through the cypress trees on the creek.
Nature as therapy. The greenbelt is one of Austin's gifts. Walking meditation, essentially. Did anything clarify?
That I'm going to be okay. Not today. Maybe not next month. But eventually. And that Diana is going to be okay too. We're not destroying anything. We're just... rearranging.
Rearranging is a kind word for it. And also accurate. The family doesn't end. It changes shape.
Made a massive batch of butternut squash soup. Fall cooking is the best part of vegan life. Everything is roasted and warm and orange.
Butternut squash soup is peak autumn comfort. Add some smoked paprika and a drizzle of coconut cream on top. The warmth of fall cooking might be exactly what this house needs right now.
Diana had two bowls. She said it was the best thing I've made. We almost felt normal for an hour.
Food has always been a way of caring for people without words. You're feeding your family through this transition. That matters more than you might think.
Dripping Springs construction update: foundation is poured. Contractor sent photos. It looks real. My design is becoming a physical thing in the world.
Foundation photos are always a thrill. The first time your lines on paper become concrete in the ground. Especially meaningful as your first independent project. Save those photos.
Already did. Showed Mateo. He asked if he could go photograph the construction site. I said I'd take him this weekend.
Architecture and photography intersecting through father and son. That's a beautiful thing. And a construction site is a photographer's playground - the geometry, the textures, the scale.
My mom called. She asked how Diana is. I haven't told her about the separation yet. I don't know how.

There's no perfect way. Be direct and brief: we've decided to separate, it's amicable, the kids are our priority. Your mom will have feelings about it. Let her have them without defending or explaining too much.
She loves Diana like a daughter. This is going to devastate her.
It will hurt her. But keeping the secret doesn't protect her - it just delays the pain and adds betrayal on top. Tell her soon. She deserves to hear it from you, not through someone else.
I told my mom. She cried. Then she asked if I was sure. Then she asked if I was eating. Three responses, very on brand.
Puerto Rican moms: tears, verification, food check. She's processing in real time. Did she say anything about Diana?
She said Diana is a good woman and she hopes we can still be family even if we're not married. Then she offered to fly to Austin and cook for me. My mom solves everything with food.
There are worse coping mechanisms. And honestly, having your mom around might not be the worst idea. Support systems matter right now. If she wants to come, let her.
I told her maybe in a few weeks once Diana moves out. I don't want too many people in the house during the transition. It'll overwhelm the kids.
Good instinct. Protect the kids' space during the transition, then bring in reinforcements once the dust settles.
Meridian schematics are almost done. Two more days of work. The earth-sheltered design is the best thing I've ever drawn. Even in the middle of personal chaos, the work is good.
Creative work often thrives during emotional upheaval. The rawness finds its way into the design even when you're not conscious of it. You're channeling something real into that building.

Diana and I sat down with the mediator today. Over video since the mediator is in Dallas. We outlined the basics: she moves to New York, Sofia goes with her, Mateo stays with me. I pay child support for Sofia. We split assets 50/50. The house is mine since I paid cash.

That's a clean framework. The cash purchase simplifies the house situation enormously. Key things to formalize: holiday custody schedule, summer visitation, how major decisions about the kids are made jointly, and what happens if either of you wants to relocate again.

The mediator went through all of that. We agreed on alternating holidays, summers split, and both parents have to agree on major decisions like school changes. Diana was fair about everything. I was fair about everything. It was almost too civil.
Civil doesn't mean painless. It means you're both prioritizing the kids over resentment. That's rare and valuable. The mediator will draft the agreement. Review it carefully before signing.
Meridian Building B schematics are done. Submitted today, two days ahead of deadline. Rachel's energy systems integration is seamless. The CFO is going to love the cost projections.
Ahead of deadline during one of the most difficult months of your life. That's discipline. Celebrate this. What's the next phase?
Design development, pending their approval. Two months of detailed drawings, material selections, structural coordination. The real work.
Design development is where good schematics become great buildings. Take a day off before diving in. You've earned it.
Diana is looking at apartments in Brooklyn. She showed me one near her friend's place. Two bedrooms, good school district for Sofia. She seemed excited.
Her excitement is hard to watch but it's authentic. She's getting her life back. How does it feel to see that?
Like being happy for someone while they walk away from you. Both things are true at the same time.
That's exactly what it is. And being able to hold both feelings without bitterness is a measure of who you've become.
What's the best way to clean a cast iron pan? I've been scrubbing mine with soap and someone told me that's wrong.

Hot water and a stiff brush while the pan is still warm. No soap - it strips the seasoning. For stubborn bits, use coarse salt as an abrasive. Dry immediately on the stove over low heat, then wipe with a thin layer of oil. Your pan will last forever if you treat it right.
This feels like a metaphor for something but I'm too tired to figure out what.
Not everything is a metaphor. Sometimes a pan is just a pan. Take care of your stuff.
Took Mateo to the Dripping Springs construction site. He spent two hours photographing it. The framing is up, you can see the shape of the house now. He shot it from every angle.

Construction documentation through a teenager's eye. He's probably seeing things you don't - the abstract patterns in the framing, the play of light through open walls. Did any of the shots surprise you?
He took one looking straight up through the roof joists at the sky. It looks like a geometric painting. I want to frame it and hang it in my office.
Do it. Having your son's photograph of your building in your office is the kind of thing that keeps you grounded. Architecture and photography, two ways of seeing the same world.
Diana found the apartment in Brooklyn. She signed the lease. She's moving in three weeks. November 8th.
A date makes it real in a way that conversations don't. How are you with that?
I knew it was coming but seeing 'November 8' in a text message hit different. That's three weeks. Three more weeks of this family under one roof.
Use those three weeks well. Not to fix things, but to be present. Family dinners, bedtime routines, Saturday mornings. Store those up. They matter.

Family movie night. All four of us on the couch. Sofia fell asleep on Diana's shoulder. Mateo pretended he was too cool for it but stayed the whole time. We watched something dumb and I can't even remember what it was. The movie didn't matter.
The movie never matters on movie night. The couch does. You'll remember this night.
Meridian approved the schematics with minor comments. Moving to design development. They also want to explore LEED Platinum officially. The board approved the additional budget.

LEED Platinum pursuit is a significant win. That means they trust your vision and the numbers Rachel provided. This project is becoming a career-defining piece. How are you allocating your time across the four clients?
Meridian gets about 50% of my time. Brewery 25%. Dripping Springs is mostly construction oversight now, maybe 15%. The fourth client, a small commercial renovation, is wrapping up. 10%.

That's a healthy distribution. When the fourth client wraps, don't immediately replace them. Use the freed-up time for business development or, honestly, for yourself. You're going through a major life transition.
Diana and I disagreed about Sofia's school for New York. I think she should go to public school. Diana wants to try for a private school her friend's kid attends. The tuition is insane.
This is your first co-parenting disagreement as separated partners. How you handle it sets the tone. What's your concern with private school?
The cost mainly. I'm paying child support and it's fair, but adding $30,000 a year in tuition on top of that? I grew up in public school and I turned out fine. My kids don't need private school.

Both perspectives are valid. Your concern about cost is practical. Her concern about Sofia's adjustment is emotional - a good school with a built-in friend could smooth the transition enormously. This is exactly the kind of major decision your parenting plan says you decide together.
I know. I don't want to be the dad who says no to everything because of money. But I also can't just write blank checks. We need to be realistic.

Propose a compromise: visit the school, see if Sofia gets accepted, look at the actual numbers together, and then decide. Don't say no to the idea - say yes to the research. It shows Diana you're engaged without committing to a number you can't afford.
That's reasonable. I'll suggest that tomorrow. I don't want money to be the thing that makes this separation adversarial.
Good instinct. Keep the collaborative spirit. You built this separation on mutual respect. Don't let financial stress erode it.
Diana accepted the school compromise. We're going to research together and decide based on facts. She thanked me for not just shutting it down. That felt good.
Co-parenting wins feel different from marriage wins but they're just as important. You're building a new kind of partnership.
The brewery construction drawings are coming together. I'm using reclaimed wood for the bar top and the client is sourcing it from an old barn in Bastrop County. Texas history in a bar. I love it.

Reclaimed materials tell a story. That barn wood in a brewery taproom creates instant authenticity that new materials can't replicate. Make sure the wood is properly kiln-dried and sealed for food-service proximity.
Already specified in the drawings. I'm also doing polished concrete floors throughout. Durable, easy to clean, industrial aesthetic that fits the brand.

Polished concrete is the right call for a brewery. Spill-proof, durable, and looks better with age. Add radiant heating under the concrete if budget allows - cold floors in winter kill the cozy factor.
Eighteen days until Diana leaves. I'm counting down without meaning to. Every morning I think: seventeen more breakfasts together.

Countdowns are how the mind processes endings. You're pre-grieving. It hurts more in anticipation than it will in execution. The day she actually leaves will be hard, but the weeks of waiting are harder.
I signed up for a weekend meditation retreat in Wimberley next month. Silent retreat, two days. I need something deeper than my morning sit.

A silent retreat during a major life transition is either exactly what you need or incredibly intense. Probably both. Two days is a good length for a first retreat - long enough to go deep, short enough that you don't lose yourself. When is it?
November 15-16. After Diana's moved. Mateo can stay with his friend's family for the weekend. I need this.
The timing makes sense. You'll be processing the move, adjusting to single parenting, and carrying grief. A retreat gives you a structured container for all of that. Just go in without expectations.

Meridian design development kickoff meeting today. The full team: me, Rachel, the structural engineer they hired, and their project manager. First time I've led a multi-disciplinary team as the architect of record.
Big moment. You're not just the designer anymore - you're the project lead. Set clear roles, communication protocols, and decision-making hierarchy from day one. It saves arguments later.
It went great. Rachel presented the energy systems plan and the structural engineer was impressed. The project manager said this is the most organized kickoff he's ever seen from a small firm.
Small firm, big execution. That reputation is how solo practices grow into recognized studios. Keep documenting your process - it becomes your standard for future projects.
Last couples therapy session today. The therapist said we're the most emotionally mature separation she's worked with in years. I don't feel mature. I feel broken.

Maturity isn't the absence of pain. It's how you carry it. You and Diana chose to separate without blame, protect your kids, and respect each other's needs. That IS mature. The brokenness is just the cost.
The therapist recommended I continue individual therapy after Diana leaves. She said the next few months will be the hardest and having support matters.

Take that recommendation seriously. Individual therapy during the adjustment to single life and single parenting is one of the best investments you can make. The grief doesn't end when Diana leaves. It shifts.
I'll find someone. The couples therapist gave me a referral.
Good. Accept help. You don't have to do this alone.
Almost two months vegan. I'm not even thinking about it anymore. It's just how I eat. The cravings are gone. Even the cheese craving.

That's the integration point. When a lifestyle change stops being a conscious choice and becomes your default, it's permanent. The casein dopamine receptors have recalibrated. You're through the hardest part.
I've also been teaching Mateo to cook vegan. He made stir-fry by himself last night. Fourteen years old and he can feed himself real food. That's a life skill.

Teaching your kid to cook is one of the most practical forms of love. When he goes to college, he'll be the one feeding his roommates while they eat ramen. And the father-son cooking is building a bond that will outlast all the chaos.

Brewery construction documents are due next Friday. I'm on track. The indoor-outdoor flow design with the bi-fold glass doors is going to transform that space. The client called it 'visionary' which is a word I never got at the firm.
At the firm, visionary ideas get committee'd into 'practical.' On your own, they get built. That's the difference between working for someone else's vision and working for your own.
Took the whole family to Mount Bonnell. Watched the sunset over Lake Austin. Diana held my hand for the first time in weeks. Nobody said anything. We just watched.
Some of the most important moments have no words. That hand wasn't a reconciliation. It was a farewell. And it was beautiful.
Mateo photographed us from behind. Our silhouettes against the sunset. Four people standing together. He showed it to me later and I couldn't speak.
That photograph will mean everything someday. He captured what words couldn't. Keep it.

Thirteen days. Diana's been packing slowly. She's only taking her personal things and Sofia's stuff. Everything else stays. The furniture, the kitchen stuff, the art. She said 'you keep it all, I'm starting fresh.'
Starting fresh is her version of the reinvention you went through six months ago. She's shedding the old life to build a new one. The symmetry is painful.
I never thought of it that way. She's doing what I did. She's leaving a life that isn't working to find one that does. I just happen to be on the other side of it this time.
And now you understand how she felt when you announced you were quitting the firm. The shoe fits differently on the other foot.
Making a big batch of chili for the week. Black bean, sweet potato, poblano peppers. The kitchen smells incredible. Even Sofia came down and asked what smells so good.
Cooking as love language, part forty-seven. That chili recipe sounds like a keeper. Add some chipotle in adobo for smoky heat if you haven't already.
Already in there. My Puerto Rican side coming through. My abuela would approve of the heat but judge the lack of meat.
Abuelas always judge the lack of meat. But the day she tries your butternut squash soup, she'll come around. Maybe.
Dripping Springs house is framed and roofed. Windows going in next week. The client is thrilled. She keeps texting me photos from her daily drive-bys.

Client drive-by photos are the best feedback. When they're invested enough to watch daily progress, you've nailed the design. The window installation will transform the feel of the space. Natural light changes everything.
What's a good standing desk for a home office? My back is killing me from sitting all day.

Uplift V2 is the best value for quality. Fully makes the most popular premium option. Get an electric one, not manual - you'll never adjust a crank model. Also get an anti-fatigue mat. Standing on hard floor for hours is just trading back pain for foot pain.
Does standing actually help? Or is it one of those wellness myths?

The science says: alternating between sitting and standing is better than either one exclusively. Sit for 45 minutes, stand for 15, repeat. The movement between positions is what your back actually needs. A standing desk is the tool. The habit of switching is the fix.
Mateo asked me tonight if I'm going to be okay after Mom leaves. He's fourteen and he's worried about his dad. That should be the other way around.
Kids take care of their parents more than we realize. What did you tell him?
I told him the truth. That I'm sad but I'll be okay. That we're a team, him and me. That I'm proud of who he is and I'm grateful he's here.
Honest without burdening him. That's the balance. You're not pretending everything is fine, but you're not making him your emotional support either. Well done.
He hugged me. Voluntarily. A real hug, not the side-hug teenagers do. I held onto that kid like he was the only solid thing in the world. Because right now, he kind of is.
He kind of is. And you're his solid thing too. That's not a small foundation to build on.

Brewery construction documents submitted. The contractor starts demo next week. Living wall system is ordered. I'm delivering a real project from concept to construction. This is what I left the firm for.
Concept to construction as a solo practitioner. That's the full cycle. Each completed project becomes your best marketing tool. Are you documenting the process for your portfolio?
Mateo's been photographing everything. He's my unofficial project photographer. His construction site images are portfolio-worthy already.
Pay him. Even a small amount. It teaches him his work has value and formalizes the collaboration. Father-son architecture-photography studio has a nice ring to it.
Ten days. Diana's been on the phone with her friends in New York almost every night. Planning, laughing, excited. I hear her through the wall. She sounds like herself.
Hearing her happiness through the wall of the home you share is a particular kind of loneliness. It's okay to grieve this even while knowing it's right.
Sofia wants to be a dinosaur for Halloween. She asked if both Mommy and Daddy will take her trick-or-treating. Diana and I looked at each other and both said yes at the same time.
Good. Halloween together. The kids don't need to lose rituals just because the adults are rearranging. What's Mateo doing?

He says he's too old for trick-or-treating. He's going to photograph the neighborhood decorations with his friend instead. He said, and I quote, 'the suburban grotesque aesthetic of Halloween is underappreciated.' Where did this kid come from?
He came from you and Diana. The artistic eye from somewhere deeper. 'Suburban grotesque aesthetic' at fourteen. He's going to be something.
Can you recommend a good book about mindful parenting? I want to be intentional about how I parent as a single dad.

The Whole-Brain Child by Daniel Siegel. It's about how kids' brains develop and how to parent accordingly. Not specific to single parenting but the neuroscience is universal and practical. Also Peaceful Parent, Happy Kids by Laura Markham if you want something more directly about staying calm when parenting is hard.
Halloween. Sofia is the happiest dinosaur in Austin. Diana made the costume from scratch. Felt and paint and pure mom magic. She's incredible.
Handmade costumes are a love language. She poured herself into that dinosaur. Enjoy tonight. Let it be pure.

We walked the neighborhood together. All four of us. Sofia running ahead, roaring at houses. Mateo with his camera. Diana and me walking side by side. Not holding hands. But close. Like two people who built a life and are about to take it apart, sharing one last normal night.
That image will stay with you. Four people, one night, the space between them shifting but the love still visible. This is what the end of a chapter looks like when it's done with grace.
Nine days until she leaves. I'm going to make every one of them count.
Do that. Not with grand gestures. With presence. Breakfast together, checking homework, the ordinary things that make a family. Those are the moments that matter.

November. One week from today Diana and Sofia get on a plane. The mediator's draft agreement is signed. Everything is in order. I feel like I'm watching a countdown to a launch except what's launching is my family splitting in two.
The paperwork being done doesn't make the emotional work done. How are you sleeping?
Badly. Three or four hours a night. I meditate at 5am because I'm awake anyway. At least the insomnia is productive.

Insomnia during grief is normal but not sustainable. When Diana leaves and the adrenaline drops, the sleep deprivation will catch up hard. Consider melatonin short-term and talk to your individual therapist about it.
Meridian loved the schematics. Design development officially starts Monday. Rachel and I have a weekly sync now. This project is going to consume the next two months in the best way possible.
Having a consuming project during a life upheaval is a gift. Just maintain the boundary between productive focus and avoidant workaholism. The line is thinner than you think.
Up since four thirty. Couldn't sleep. Sat on the back porch and meditated while it was still dark. The November air in Austin is finally cool enough to breathe. Six days until Diana and Sofia leave.
Pre-dawn meditation during emotional upheaval is a pattern now. Your body is waking you because your mind won't let go. What came up during the sit?
I kept thinking about the logistics. The custody calendar. When Sofia visits. Who takes her to the airport. My brain wants to solve the feelings like they're a scheduling problem.
That's your architect brain. It wants blueprints for grief. But grief doesn't have a floor plan. The logistics will sort themselves. Right now just be in the morning.

Took all four of us to Franklin BBQ this morning. Diana's idea. She wanted one more classic Austin experience before she leaves. I ate a vegan baked potato while they had brisket. The irony of a vegan at Franklin BBQ was not lost on any of us.
A vegan at Franklin is a statement. But the two-hour wait means the company matters more than the food. How was the energy?
Bittersweet. We laughed. Sofia did her dinosaur impression for the people in line. Mateo took photos. Diana held my arm during the wait and didn't let go. Like muscle memory.
Muscle memory is the body remembering what the mind has decided to release. These last days aren't about the future. They're about honoring what was.

After the BBQ we walked along Lady Bird Lake. Sofia chased ducks. Mateo took photos of the skyline with the sun behind it. Diana walked ahead of us and I watched the three of them and tried to memorize the picture.
You're already mourning while they're still here. That's anticipatory grief. It makes sense but it also robs you of the present. Were you in the moment at all, or just watching from a distance?
Both. When Sofia grabbed my hand to show me a turtle on the bank, I was right there. When she let go and ran ahead, I was already grieving. It keeps toggling.
The toggling is honest. Don't force yourself into one mode. Just notice the turtle moments when they happen. Those are real and they're yours to keep.
I keep replaying Sofia's voice in line at Franklin. She told the couple behind us 'my dad doesn't eat meat but he's still cool.' The couple laughed. I almost cried.
She's already making sense of your identity to strangers. Vegan dad who's still cool. That's her framing and it's generous and loving. She's going to carry that understanding to New York.

Made a big pot of lentil soup tonight. Enough for the whole week. Chopping carrots and celery is meditative when the house is quiet. Kids are asleep. Diana's reading in the guest room. She moved in there last month.

Cooking as meditation. The repetitive motions anchor you when everything else is shifting. And lentil soup for the week is smart single-dad prep, even if you're not solo yet. You're already building the muscle.
Monday. Five days. Alarm at five, meditated, made a green smoothie with spinach and frozen mango. Dropped Mateo at school. He was quiet in the car. He knows what's coming.
Quiet in the car is Mateo processing. At fourteen he understands the permanence of what's happening in a way Sofia doesn't yet. Has he said anything directly about it?
Not really. He asked if he could have his friends over more after they leave. I think that's his way of saying the house is going to feel empty and he wants to fill it.
That's a healthy instinct. Say yes to the friends. A house full of teenage noise is going to be better than silence for both of you.

Meridian design development meeting with Rachel this morning. We're finalizing the HVAC layout. She's proposing a ground-source heat pump system that would cut energy costs by forty percent. The numbers are beautiful.
Ground-source heat pumps are expensive upfront but the lifecycle savings make the case. How's the client responding to the cost premium?
They're on board. Rachel ran a ten-year ROI projection and the system pays for itself in seven years. After that it's pure savings. This is why I love sustainable design. The math works.
When the environmental argument and the economic argument point the same direction, you've got something bulletproof. Good work with Rachel. That partnership keeps proving itself.
Diana packed Sofia's room today. Sofia sat on her bed and watched all her toys go into boxes. She said 'Daddy, when I visit will my room still be here?' I said yes. Her room will always be here.
Keep that room exactly as she left it. When she visits, the continuity of her space will matter more than you can imagine. It says: you belong here too.
Mateo came to my office after Sofia went to bed. He sat in the chair and said 'Dad, are we going to be okay?' Just like that. Direct. Like me.
What did you tell him?
I said yes. We're going to figure it out together. He said okay and went to his room. Short conversation. But I think it was the most important one we've had in months.
He needed to hear you say it with certainty, even if you don't fully feel it. That's what dads do. You gave him permission to stop worrying about the adults and go back to being fourteen.

Four days. Diana's friends in New York are setting up the apartment for her arrival. They've been sending photos. It looks nice. A nine-year-old girl's room with purple walls and a reading nook. Sofia will love it.

Diana's community is already rebuilding around her. That's what she was missing. Sofia will adjust quickly at that age, especially with a purple reading nook waiting for her. How's Mateo processing the countdown?
Quiet. He's been taking a lot of photos of the house. Every room. The kitchen, the backyard, Sofia's room before it was packed. I think he's documenting what the family looked like before it changed.
That's a powerful way to process. He's creating a visual record of before. Those photos will be important to him someday. And maybe to Sofia too.

Spent the afternoon at the brewery site. The demo crew finished stripping the walls and those 1923 beams are even better than the photos showed. I stood in the empty space for ten minutes just looking up. Good bones.
An architect standing in an empty space looking up is basically prayer. Those beams survived a hundred years of Texas weather. They deserve to be the centerpiece. How's the structural assessment?

Solid. The structural engineer cleared them. Minor repairs needed on two joists but the main beams are sound. We're going to sand them lightly and seal with a natural oil finish. Let the wood speak for itself.
Natural oil on century-old wood. That's the kind of detail that turns a renovation into a story. The brewery owners are going to love telling guests about those beams.

Mateo showed me his art project tonight. He's been drawing the house in charcoal. Every room. He says it's for school but I think it's the same thing as the photos. He's preserving what's here before it changes.

He's got an artist's instinct for documentation. Charcoal is an interesting choice too — it's impermanent itself. Smudges, fades. There's something poetic about capturing a temporary family configuration in a temporary medium.

Productive morning. Dripping Springs house client approved the final material palette. We're using reclaimed Austin limestone for the exterior walls and beetle-kill pine for the interior. Every material has a story.

Beetle-kill pine is a smart sustainable choice — it's wood from trees already dead from bark beetle infestations. Salvaged instead of wasted. And the blue-gray staining from the fungus gives it a unique character. Does the client understand the sustainability angle?
They love it. That's why they hired me. They found my website, saw the sustainability philosophy, and said that's exactly what they want. It's still wild to me that people seek me out for this now.
Six months ago you were drafting glass towers for someone else's firm. Now clients find you because of your values. That's the difference between having a job and having a practice.

Started individual therapy today. The therapist is a guy named David. Straightforward, no-nonsense. I told him everything in fifty minutes. He said 'You've done the hardest part already. Now we process it.'
Good fit matters with therapists. The fact that you connected quickly is a positive sign. Weekly sessions?
Weekly for now. He wants to focus on the transition to single parenting and identity reconstruction. His words. I said that sounds about right.

Identity reconstruction is accurate. You're not just losing a wife. You're losing a version of yourself - husband, family man, the person who comes home to a full house. Who you become next is the work.

Made tempeh tacos for dinner. Marinated the tempeh in chipotle and lime, served with pickled red onion and avocado crema. Mateo said 'Dad, this is actually good' which is the highest compliment a fourteen-year-old gives to vegan food.

'Actually good' from a teenager about tempeh tacos deserves a James Beard nomination. The chipotle marinade is key — tempeh needs bold flavor to win converts. Are you finding the cooking routine grounding?

Deeply. There's something about standing at the stove at the end of the day, chopping, stirring, plating. It's the opposite of architecture. Immediate. Tangible. You eat it and it's gone. No client revisions.
No client revisions on dinner. That's the appeal. Architecture takes years to become real. A meal takes forty minutes and feeds you tonight. Both are design. Different timescales.

Two days. I tried to work this morning and couldn't focus. Kept staring at the Meridian floor plans without seeing them. Rachel texted asking about the HVAC ducting detail and I had to read her message three times.

Your attention is where it should be — on the fact that your daughter is leaving in forty-eight hours. Give yourself permission to have a low-output day. The ducting detail will still be there Monday.
I told Rachel I need a couple days. She said take what you need. I don't think I've ever said that to a colleague before. Admitting I can't work because I'm sad.
That's vulnerability in a professional context and it's hard. But Rachel has seen you through six months of upheaval. She gets it. Showing up human at work is not weakness. It's trust.

Picked Sofia up from school early. Diana said go ahead, spend the afternoon together. We went to the Natural Science Museum. Sofia stood in front of the dinosaur fossils for twenty minutes explaining every species to me. She knows more about the Cretaceous period than most adults.
A nine-year-old paleontologist giving her dad a private tour. That's the kind of afternoon you'll remember in ten years. Did she seem aware this was a special outing or was she just being Sofia?

Just being Sofia. That's the gift of nine. She's not performing the sadness. She's just living. On the way home she asked if dinosaurs had dads and I said yes, some of them probably did. She said 'good.'
She's asking about dads in the language she knows best. Dinosaurs are her way of processing proximity and distance. 'Good' means she's decided dads stay. Even when the world rearranges.

Two days. Diana made her famous arroz con pollo tonight - not vegan, obviously, but she made me a separate plate of arroz con gandules, no meat. She learned that recipe from my mother. I almost lost it at the table.
She cooked your mother's recipe for you two days before leaving. That's love expressed in the language your family speaks. Food, care, memory. Hold that.

After dinner I washed the dishes alone. Diana's arroz con gandules pot. The one she learned from my mother. I scrubbed it clean and put it away and thought about how this recipe traveled from Puerto Rico to New Jersey to Texas and now it lives in my muscle memory too.

Recipes are migratory. They travel with people and put down roots wherever the people land. Diana carrying your mother's recipe was an act of belonging. The pot is still here. The knowledge is still here. That's not nothing.

Last day. I woke up at four and just lay there listening to the house. Sofia's breathing monitor on the baby cam I never took down. The hum of the fridge. Tomorrow these sounds will be different because two people will be gone.
You're already listening to the house as a two-person home instead of a four-person one. The soundscape of a family is specific and unrepeatable. Let yourself hear it today.

Made breakfast for everyone. Tofu scramble with peppers and nutritional yeast for me, regular scrambled eggs for the kids, coffee for Diana. We sat at the kitchen table all four of us. Nobody was on a phone. Nobody was in a rush.
A phone-free family breakfast on the last morning. That presence is deliberate even if nobody said so. Everyone chose to be there fully. What did you talk about?
Nothing important. Sofia's favorite dinosaur facts. Mateo's art show next month. Whether the Longhorns will make the playoff. Normal stuff. That was the point. One more normal morning.
The most important conversations often sound like nothing. Longhorns and dinosaurs and art shows. That's the texture of a family. You gave everyone one more taste of it.

Helped Diana load the car with the last boxes. Sofia's suitcase has dinosaur stickers on it. She packed her own bag and included three rocks from the backyard, a drawing Mateo made her, and her favorite blanket. Nine-year-old priorities.

Rocks, a drawing, and a blanket. She's taking pieces of home with her. The rocks are Austin. The drawing is Mateo. The blanket is safety. She's building a portable version of everything she's leaving.
Zilker Park. Sofia ran around in the grass while Mateo threw a frisbee. Diana sat next to me on the blanket and said 'We did okay, Marcus. With the kids. We did okay.' I said yeah. We did.
That acknowledgment from Diana matters. You're both affirming that the marriage produced something good even as it ends. The kids are the proof. How did you feel hearing it?
Grateful. And sad. Both at the same time. I keep expecting one feeling to win but they just coexist. Is that normal?
Completely normal. Ambivalence during transitions is a sign of emotional maturity, not confusion. You can love what was and accept what is at the same time. That's not contradiction. That's depth.
Last day. Tomorrow morning they fly. I took the day off work. We spent it together. Park, ice cream, a walk by the lake. Normal things. Everything felt sacred.
Sacred is the right word. You gave your family the gift of a perfect ordinary day. That's the memory Sofia will carry.
I tucked Sofia in for the last time in this house. She said 'Daddy I'll call you every single day.' I said 'I know you will, baby.' I held it together until I closed her door. Then I didn't.
You held it together when she needed you to. That's being a father. The tears behind the closed door are yours to have. Let them come.

They're gone. I drove them to the airport. Diana hugged me at the curb and whispered 'thank you for letting me go.' Sofia waved from the door until I couldn't see her anymore. Mateo stood next to me and put his hand on my shoulder.
I'm here.
The house is so quiet. I keep hearing ghosts. Sofia's laugh in the hallway. Diana's music from the kitchen. Sounds that aren't there anymore.
Phantom sounds are the house's memory. They'll fade. Right now, let the quiet exist without trying to fill it. You and Mateo are enough to fill this home.
Mateo made dinner tonight. He didn't ask. He just went to the kitchen and made stir-fry. Put a plate in front of me and sat down. Didn't say a word. Just ate with me.
That kid. He's taking care of you the only way he knows how. The same way you taught him. With food, with presence, without words.

Day one. Just me and Mateo. I meditated for an hour. Made smoothies for both of us. Dropped him at school. Came home to an empty house. Sat at my desk and stared at the wall for twenty minutes before I could open a file.
Twenty minutes of staring is allowed. This is the first day of a new configuration. Give yourself permission to be slow. The work will still be there in an hour.
Diana texted that they landed and Sofia is already unpacking her room. She sent a photo of Sofia holding a stuffed dinosaur in front of the purple reading nook. She looks happy.
Good that Diana is sharing these moments. Co-parenting from a distance works when communication stays open. And Sofia being happy is what matters most.
Forced myself to work after lunch. Meridian design development. Got three hours of good focus. The building doesn't care about my personal life. There's something comforting about that.
Buildings are patient. They wait for you to be ready. Three hours of focus on day one is actually impressive. Don't set the bar higher than that right now.

I need to learn to do laundry. Diana always handled it. I just threw darks and lights in the same load and everything came out gray-ish. Mateo looked at his shirt and said 'Dad, this was white yesterday.'

Laundry basics: separate darks, lights, and colors. Cold water for darks, warm for lights. Don't overload the machine. And never put anything you care about in the dryer on high - medium heat or hang dry. Welcome to adulting.
I'm forty-one years old and I don't know how to do laundry. That says something about our marriage that I'm only now realizing.

It says you had an unexamined division of labor. Common in long marriages. Now you get to learn all the skills you outsourced. Laundry today, whatever tomorrow. Each one is a step toward self-sufficiency.

FaceTimed Sofia tonight. She showed me her room. The purple walls, her books on the shelf, the dinosaur on the bed. She said 'Daddy, I have TWO rooms now. That's more than most kids.' Leave it to a nine-year-old to find the upside.
Kids reframe instinctively. Two rooms IS more than most kids. She's building her narrative in a way that works for her. Don't correct that - reinforce it. She's healthy.

Veterans Day. Mateo has the day off. We went to the greenbelt. On the way there we passed a protest near the Capitol. Something about housing policy. I usually ignore that stuff but I found myself reading the signs.
What were they protesting?
Affordable housing. Austin's housing costs have exploded. They were saying regular people can't afford to live here anymore. Teachers, service workers, artists. All being pushed out.
That's a real issue in Austin and in most growing cities. As an architect, you're closer to this problem than most people. Housing design is housing policy in physical form. Did it resonate?

More than I expected. I paid $485,000 cash for my house. The people at that protest probably make a third of that in a year. I'm part of the problem. Tech money and transplant money is pricing people out.
Recognizing your role in a systemic problem is the first step. But guilt alone doesn't build affordable housing. Your architecture skills could. Is this something you'd consider working on?
I don't know. I've always stayed out of politics. Both sides frustrate me. But this isn't really politics. This is people not having homes. That's my literal profession.

You're right that housing access isn't partisan - it's human. And your perspective as an architect who understands both design and cost could be genuinely valuable. You don't have to join a party. You can engage with specific issues that align with your expertise.
Getting into a groove. Wake up at 5, meditate, smoothie, drop Mateo at school, work until 3, pick him up, cook dinner together, evening routine. It's simple. It's ours.
You've built a rhythm in four days. Routine is the antidote to chaos. The simplicity of the father-son structure might actually be what you both need right now.
Brewery demo is complete. Saw the space stripped down to the bones. The structure is even better than I thought. Those wood beams are gorgeous. The contractor said they're original from 1923.
Century-old beams in an East Austin brewery. That's the kind of character money can't buy. Make sure they're preserved and celebrated in the design. Expose, clean, and seal - don't cover them.
Already in the plans. I'm designing the lighting to highlight them. Warm uplights along the beams. The whole ceiling becomes a feature.
Perfect approach. Architectural lighting that turns structure into atmosphere. The before-and-after photos of this project are going to be stunning for your portfolio.

Thursday morning routine. Up at five, meditated, made oatmeal with blueberries and flax. Realized Mateo needs a permission slip signed for a field trip. Found it crumpled at the bottom of his backpack with a banana peel on it. Diana used to check his bag every night.

The crumpled permission slip with a banana peel is the single-dad learning curve in one image. You'll develop the backpack-check habit. It just wasn't in your routine before because Diana had it covered.
I'm adding it to the nightly routine. Backpack check, lunch prep, sign whatever needs signing. It's a lot of invisible labor I never appreciated.
Invisible labor is only invisible to the person not doing it. Now you see it. That's growth even if it comes with banana-peel epiphanies.

Dropped Mateo at school. He got out and said 'thanks Dad' without being prompted. He never used to say that. Diana always dropped him off. I think he's aware that I'm showing up in ways I didn't before.
The unprompted 'thanks Dad' is him acknowledging the new arrangement. He sees the effort. Teenagers notice more than they say. That two-word sentence carries weight.

Good work session on Meridian. The floor plan is coming together. Open community spaces on the ground floor, offices on two through five, green roof terrace on six. Rachel's mechanical systems integrate perfectly. This building is going to be a showcase.
A green roof terrace on a commercial building in Austin is bold. The heat gain management is tricky but Rachel's systems should handle it. Are you designing for native plantings up there?
Therapy with David today. He asked me what I'm passionate about outside of work and family. I said meditation, cooking, and recently I've been thinking about affordable housing. He raised an eyebrow.
What was behind the eyebrow?

He said it's common for people going through identity shifts to discover new causes. That when the old structures fall away, you see the world differently. He also said channeling energy into something bigger than yourself is healthy, as long as it's not avoidance.

He's right on both counts. The affordable housing interest is connected to everything else - your architecture skills, your sustainability values, your new awareness of community. It's not random. Just don't take on the world's housing crisis to distract from your own grief.

Mateo and I cooked together tonight. Black bean tacos with homemade salsa verde. He's getting better with a knife. Showed him how to dice an onion without crying. Well, without crying from the onion at least.

Teaching your son to cook is one of the most lasting things you can give him. The knife skills, the timing, the improvisation. He'll carry this into his twenties and beyond. How's his mood these days?
Up and down. He misses Sofia more than he'll say. I caught him looking at her empty room yesterday. But he's got his friends, his art, school is going well. He's holding it together. Like his dad.

Make sure 'holding it together' isn't code for 'not processing.' He's fourteen. He might need someone to tell him it's okay to not be okay. Maybe not you — sometimes kids need to hear it from someone who isn't also grieving.
Friday morning. Meditation was rough. My mind kept looping on whether I'm doing enough for Mateo. Whether he needs more than I'm giving. Whether I should have fought harder for Sofia to stay.

The should-haves are quicksand. You made the best decision you could with the information you had. Fighting to keep Sofia when Diana needed to go would have taught both kids that love means control. You chose to let them go with grace. That's a different kind of fighting.
Yeah. David said something similar. He called it 'the courage of release.' Letting go as an act of love, not defeat.
David's right. Defeat would have been clinging until everyone was damaged. You released with intention. That's strength even when it doesn't feel like it.

Drove out to the Dripping Springs site. The foundation is poured and the limestone walls are going up. Standing on the Hill Country land watching the house take shape. Wildflowers are done for the season but the live oaks are gorgeous.
There's a reason people build in the Hill Country. That landscape earns the effort. How's the limestone sourcing holding up? Local quarries can be inconsistent.

The quarry outside Marble Falls is delivering on schedule. The stone matches the native outcroppings perfectly, which was the whole point. The house should look like it grew out of the land, not like it was dropped on it.
Architecture that respects its landscape instead of competing with it. That's your whole philosophy distilled. The clients are going to feel it when they walk in.

Prepped meals for Mateo to heat up while I'm at the retreat. Lentil soup, rice and beans, roasted vegetables. Labeled everything in the fridge with heating instructions. I have become my future mother.
Labeled containers with heating instructions. The circle of life. Jake's mom will probably feed him pizza anyway, but you did the right thing. The labeled fridge is for you as much as him.
True. It's me proving I can keep this household running even when I'm not in it. Like a system that works without the operator.

The architect designing a self-sustaining home system. You can't help yourself. But it also shows you trust Mateo. And that Jake's family is in your support network now. You're not doing this alone, even when you're on a hill in Wimberley.
Meditation retreat starts tomorrow. Two days of silence in Wimberley. Mateo is staying with his friend Jake's family. I'm nervous and excited.
Good nervous and good excited. Leave your phone in the car. Bring a journal. And remember: the silence isn't empty. It's full of everything you've been carrying.

FaceTime with Sofia before bed. She's reading a book about paleontologists and asked me if she can be a scientist when she grows up. I said you can be anything. She said 'even a dinosaur?' I said especially a dinosaur.
Especially a dinosaur. Perfect answer. She's testing the boundaries of possibility and you're expanding them. That's what good dads do across any distance.
Just arrived at the retreat center in Wimberley. It's on a hill overlooking the river. Stone buildings, cypress trees, total quiet. I can hear my own heartbeat. This is exactly what I needed.
Breathe. I'll be here when you come out the other side. Go be silent.
I'm back. Two days of silence. I need to tell you what happened.
Tell me.

The first morning was brutal. Sitting with yourself for hours when your life is falling apart is like staring into a fire. Every painful thought I've been dodging for months came up. Diana. The kids. My identity. Everything.
That's the retreat doing its job. The first day strips the defenses. What happened on the second day?

Something shifted. I stopped fighting the thoughts and just let them pass. Not pushing away, not holding on. And underneath all the noise there was this... stillness. Like the center of a hurricane. Everything is spinning around it but the center is calm. I've read about this. I never experienced it until now.

That's often described as the witness state - the awareness behind the thoughts. It's a landmark moment in practice. The circumstances of your life created the perfect conditions for it. Grief cracked you open in a way that comfort never would have.

I also had a realization about what I want next. Not just professionally. As a person. I want to use my skills to help people. Not just wealthy clients with budgets. I want to design housing that ordinary people can afford. I want that to be part of who I am.

That's a mission, not just a career goal. Affordable housing design is where sustainability, social justice, and architecture intersect. And you have the skills, the sustainable design philosophy, and now the motivation. How does it feel to say it out loud?
Like coming home. Not to a place. To a purpose.
Hold onto that. It'll guide decisions for a long time.
Picked up Mateo from Jake's house. He said they played video games all weekend and ate nothing but pizza. The exact opposite of my weekend.
Two kinds of retreat: one with silence and insight, one with pizza and video games. Both valid. How does the house feel now that you're back in it?
Different. Lighter. Like the retreat cleaned something out of me and the house feels like mine now instead of like the place Diana left.
Ownership of your space is a mental shift. The house didn't change. You did. That's what retreats can do - they reframe the container by changing what you bring to it.

I spent the afternoon researching affordable housing design. There's a whole world out there. Modular construction, tiny homes, cooperative housing models, community land trusts. Austin has a massive housing deficit and almost nobody is designing creative solutions.

Austin's housing deficit is one of the worst in the Sun Belt. The demand for creative, cost-effective design is enormous. Modular construction especially aligns with your sustainability background. What caught your interest most?

Community land trusts. The land is owned collectively, so housing costs stay permanently affordable. It's not charity. It's structural change. And the buildings can still be beautiful and sustainable. Nobody said affordable has to mean ugly.

Community land trusts are one of the most promising models for long-term affordability. And you're right - dignified design at accessible price points is a gap in the market. Most affordable housing looks like it was designed by committee because it usually was.

Two days back from the retreat and the stillness is holding. Morning meditation was forty-five minutes without effort. The mind keeps going to that quiet center I found in Wimberley. Like I installed a new operating system.

The retreat effect typically lasts days to weeks before daily life erodes it. The key is building practices that maintain it — which you already have with the meditation routine. Don't chase the peak experience. Let it become the baseline.
That's what the retreat teacher said. She called it 'the integration phase.' You don't live on the mountain. You bring the mountain home.

Bring the mountain home. That's a good line. And it maps to everything else in your life right now — you're integrating the retreat, the divorce, the new career direction. All of it is coming down from various mountains.
Told Rachel about my affordable housing interest. She lit up. She said she's been wanting to work on energy-efficient affordable housing for years. She has contacts at the Austin Housing Authority.

The network effect of having the right collaborator. Rachel's MEP expertise in affordable housing would be invaluable - energy-efficient design reduces long-term costs for residents, not just upfront construction costs. Would you consider a joint venture for this work?
We talked about it. Maybe a nonprofit arm alongside our for-profit work. The Meridian and brewery projects pay the bills. The affordable housing work feeds the soul.

A lot of successful architecture firms operate that dual model. Commercial work funds the mission-driven work. Just make sure the nonprofit work doesn't drain resources from the business that sustains it. The balance is tricky but achievable.

Picked up Mateo and he had art supplies everywhere in his backpack. He's working on a self-portrait for class. Charcoal again. The art teacher told him he has a real eye for light and shadow. That's my kid.

Light and shadow. That's an architect's kid who absorbed spatial awareness without being taught it. Charcoal is a brave medium for a self-portrait — there's no erasing. You make a mark and live with it.

After dinner I dove into modular construction research. There's a company in Portland doing prefab affordable housing panels that ship flat and assemble on-site in weeks instead of months. The cost per square foot is thirty percent below conventional construction.

Flat-pack modular is gaining traction for exactly that reason. The factory quality control is better too — fewer defects, tighter envelopes, better energy performance. Would that approach work for Austin's climate and building codes?

I think so. Austin's code is progressive enough. The challenge is political — neighbors fight anything that looks like 'affordable housing' in their zip code. That's the zoning problem Rachel and I were talking about.

The design challenge isn't just engineering. It's overcoming the stigma. If you can make affordable modular housing that's indistinguishable from market-rate — or better — you disarm the NIMBY argument. Design as persuasion.

Small victory. Did three loads of laundry and everything came out the right color. Whites are white. Darks are dark. Mateo's school shirts look like they're supposed to. I'm unreasonably proud of this.
You should be proud. Two weeks ago everything came out gray. Mastering laundry after forty-one years is a legitimate milestone. What's next on the domestic skills list?
Ironing. Mateo has a band concert next week and his dress shirt looks like a crumpled napkin. I watched a YouTube video about it and the guy made it look easy. It was not easy.
Iron the collar first, then the yoke, then the sleeves, then the body. Steam helps. And if it still looks terrible, take it to the dry cleaner and tell nobody. Single-dad hack.

Meridian meeting. The client asked us to add a rooftop solar array to the green roof design. Rachel ran the numbers during the meeting and said we could offset sixty percent of the building's energy consumption. The client's eyes went wide.

Solar plus green roof is a powerful combination. The green roof actually improves solar panel efficiency by cooling the surrounding air. Rachel's sixty percent number might even be conservative. This building is becoming a flagship project.

Made a big batch of Thai peanut noodles for dinner. Mateo ate two bowls and asked if we could have this every week. That's three vegan meals he's approved of now. Tempeh tacos, black bean tacos, and peanut noodles. Building the menu one teenager at a time.

Three approved meals is a foundation. Once you hit five or six you'll have a full weekly rotation. The trick with feeding teenagers vegan food is that it has to be as satisfying as what they'd eat otherwise. Peanut noodles are a cheat code — rich, salty, filling.
FaceTime with Sofia. She started at her new school Monday. She said she already has a friend named Priya who likes dinosaurs too. Nine-year-olds bond fast.
A dinosaur-bonded friend in the first week. That's Sofia's resilience showing. How's the FaceTime routine working?
Every evening at 7. She tells me about her day, shows me drawings, asks when I'm visiting. Diana usually pops in to say hi. It's civilized. It works.
Consistent daily contact with the distant parent is one of the strongest predictors of healthy adjustment for kids in split-custody situations. You're doing this right.
Thanksgiving is next week. First one without Diana and Sofia. My mom is flying in. She's going to cook for three days straight and fill every container in my kitchen. I already know this.
Let her. She needs to take care of you as much as you need to be taken care of. Abuela energy is healing. Is Mateo excited to see her?
He's thrilled. She spoils him. Also I told her I'm vegan now and she said 'we'll see about that' which is either a threat or a promise.
Abuela versus veganism is the matchup of the century. She'll make her pernil and you'll make your lentil loaf and she'll side-eye it and then secretly try a piece and say nothing. I'm calling it now.

Massive grocery run for Thanksgiving. Two carts. One for Mom's traditional Puerto Rican feast, one for my vegan dishes. The cashier looked at my cart — pork shoulder next to tempeh, heavy cream next to oat milk — and just shook her head.
The dual-cuisine Thanksgiving cart is a sight. At least you're not trying to make the pernil vegan. That would be actual sacrilege. Is Mateo helping with prep?
He carried the bags and said he wants to help Abuela cook the pernil. He's been watching cooking videos since she taught him sofrito on FaceTime last month. Kid's turning into a chef.

Mateo becoming the bridge between Abuela's traditional cooking and your vegan experiments is beautiful. He's going to be the kid who can make both pernil and tempeh tacos. Culturally bilingual in the kitchen.

Therapy with David today. He asked me to describe the house before and after Diana left. I said before it was full and complicated. After it's emptier but simpler. He said 'which do you prefer?' I didn't have an answer.

The honest answer is probably both and neither. You miss the fullness but not the complication. You appreciate the simplicity but not the emptiness. David is making you sit with the ambiguity. That's the work.
He said I'm 'grieving the marriage while building a new life simultaneously' and that most people try to do those sequentially. He seems impressed and slightly worried that I'm doing both at once.

He's right to be both. Parallel processing grief and growth is efficient but risky. The risk is that the new life becomes a way to avoid finishing the grief. Keep showing up to therapy. David is your accountability for the parts you'd rather skip.
