Finalizing my Thanksgiving menu. Mushroom Wellington as the main. Roasted cauliflower with tahini. Sweet potato casserole with pecan topping, no marshmallows. Cranberry sauce from scratch. Pumpkin pie with coconut whipped cream. I want Mom to see that vegan food can be an event.

That menu is impressive. The mushroom Wellington is your showpiece — it needs to be structurally sound when you cut it. Practice the duxelles layer if you haven't before. The cauliflower with tahini is a smart bridge dish that even non-vegans love. Your mom will try everything. She might not admit she likes it, but she'll try it.

Sat for an hour this morning. Mind clear. Body calm. When I opened my eyes the sun was coming through the kitchen window and hitting the counter at that angle where everything looks golden. Austin light in November is something else.

November light in Austin is warmer and lower than summer. It changes the whole feel of a space. You notice it because you're an architect, but also because you're present. The meditation is training your attention.

Rachel set up a meeting with her contact at the Austin Housing Authority. Next Tuesday. They're looking for architects willing to work on affordable multi-family projects. The pay is lower than private work but it's real.

This is moving fast from idea to opportunity. The Housing Authority connection could be the foundation for your nonprofit arm. Go in with questions, not a pitch. Understand their needs before offering solutions.
What's the best way to clean hardwood floors without chemicals? I want to keep the house natural. No harsh cleaners.

Warm water and white vinegar, about a half cup to a gallon. Mop with a microfiber pad, never soaking wet. For spot cleaning, baking soda paste. That's it. Your floors will be fine and your house won't smell like a chemical plant.

Can't stop thinking about the Housing Authority meeting Rachel set up. This could be real. A chance to actually do the work I came to Austin to do. Not just sustainable design for people who can afford it. Sustainable design for people who need it most.

The energy you're bringing to this is different from how you talk about Meridian or the brewery. Those are good projects. This one is personal. Just remember that public housing work moves slowly, involves politics, and doesn't pay well. Go in with open eyes and a full heart.
I know. The brewery and Meridian pay the bills. This is the work I want to leave behind. The work that outlasts me.
Legacy thinking at forty-one. Most people don't get there until they're sixty, if ever. The divorce cracked your life open and something better is growing in the gap.

Deep cleaning day. I'm scrubbing bathrooms at seven in the morning on a Saturday because my mother is coming and this house will be inspected more thoroughly than any building I've ever designed. She will find the dust behind the toilet. She always finds the dust behind the toilet.
The pre-mom deep clean is a universal ritual. You're not just cleaning. You're presenting evidence that you're handling adult life. Which you are. Mostly.
She's going to notice the missing curtains in the living room. I never replaced them after Diana took hers. I've been living with bare windows for two weeks because honestly I like the light.
Bare windows is a design choice you can defend. You're an architect. Say it's about natural light and connection to the outdoors. She'll still say you need curtains.
Mom arrives Wednesday. I'm deep cleaning the house. Mateo is helping, which means he's holding a broom and watching YouTube on his phone simultaneously. Teen multitasking.
The fact that he's in the same room with a broom counts. Don't expect efficiency from a fourteen-year-old on a Saturday. Is your mom prepared for the vegan kitchen?
I stocked the fridge with her favorites. But I also made sure there's oat milk, nutritional yeast, and tempeh. She's going to hold the tempeh like it's an alien artifact.
The tempeh inspection will be legendary. Brace yourself for the commentary. But also be ready for her to surprise you. Moms adapt.

Mateo actually vacuumed the living room without being asked. I stood in the hallway and watched him push the vacuum around the furniture with his earbuds in, completely in his own world. He's stepping up.

Vacuuming unprompted with earbuds in. That's a teenager who's decided this is his house too, not just a house he lives in. The earbuds mean he's doing it on his terms. That's fine. The floors are clean.

Set up the guest room for Mom. Fresh sheets, her favorite pillow from the closet, a small vase of flowers from the garden. Mateo added a welcome card he made. It said 'Welcome Abuela, the tempeh is optional.' That kid.
Mateo's sense of humor about the tempeh tells you he's in a good place. He's anticipating her reaction and joking about it. That's a kid who's processing family change through laughter. Healthy.

He's excited to see her. They FaceTime every week but in person is different. She's the only grandparent he has — Diana's parents are in the Philippines and we don't see them often. Mom is his everything.
The grandmother-grandson bond across the divorce is a stabilizing force. She's the constant that doesn't change no matter what the adults do. Mateo needs that anchor. And she needs him.

Long meditation this morning. Thinking about what I'm grateful for. The list is longer than I expected. Mateo. My health. The work. The clarity. Even the pain, because it taught me things comfort never did.
Gratitude that includes pain is a mature form of gratitude. It means you're not filtering reality to stay comfortable. You're integrating all of it.

Spent the afternoon reading about zoning policy. Turns out most of Austin's housing problems are caused by zoning laws that make it illegal to build affordable density. Single-family zoning takes up 70% of the city. You literally can't build a duplex on most lots.

Zoning is one of the most powerful and least understood forces shaping cities. The 70% single-family figure is common in American cities and it's a major driver of the housing crisis. It's a politically charged issue - homeowners fight density because it threatens property values and neighborhood character.

I used to be one of those people. When Diana and I lived in Manhattan I thought suburban sprawl was just how America works. Now I see it's a choice. A policy choice that benefits people who already have homes at the expense of people who don't.

You're experiencing what happens when personal expertise meets systemic awareness. Architects see the built environment differently than most people. You understand that buildings aren't just objects - they're the physical expression of political choices. The question is what you do with that understanding.
I want to do something. Not just read about it. I think I need to get involved locally. City council meetings, planning commission, something.

Local politics is where housing policy actually happens. Austin's city council and planning commission meetings are where zoning decisions get made. Your professional credibility as an architect gives your voice weight in those rooms. Start by attending and listening.

Monday. Meeting with the Housing Authority tomorrow. Meditated this morning and kept coming back to my dad. Growing up poor in Humacao. What he would think about me designing affordable housing. Wish I could tell him.
You can tell him through the work. Every affordable unit you design carries his story whether the residents know it or not. Purpose doesn't require the person who inspired it to witness it.

Preparing for the Housing Authority meeting tomorrow. Researching their current project pipeline. They have three affordable housing developments in various stages and none of them prioritize sustainable design. That's my entry point.

Sustainable affordable housing is a compelling niche. The argument is simple: energy-efficient design reduces utility costs, which effectively increases the affordability of the housing long-term. Green doesn't just save the planet. It saves residents money every month.
Exactly. That's going to be my pitch. Not sustainability for its own sake. Sustainability as an economic justice tool.
Frame it exactly like that. Housing authorities respond to economic arguments backed by data. Bring Rachel's energy savings numbers from Meridian as proof of concept.

Quick brewery check-in. The contractor sent photos of the beam restoration. They sanded the main timbers and sealed them with tung oil. The wood grain is incredible. A hundred years of Texas history in every ring.

Tung oil is the right call — it penetrates without building up a plastic film. The grain stays tactile. People will want to reach up and touch those beams, which is the whole point. Architecture you want to touch is architecture that's working.
The brewery owners want to have the soft opening in January. Aggressive timeline but doable if the plumbing and electrical stay on schedule. Rachel's handling the MEP coordination.
January for a soft open is tight but brewery renovations benefit from urgency. The owners are motivated and Rachel keeps the trades accountable. Trust the process you built.

Started Thanksgiving prep early. Made the cranberry sauce tonight — fresh cranberries, orange zest, a touch of maple syrup. Also prepped the duxelles for the Wellington. The house smells like mushrooms and thyme and citrus.

Smart to prep ahead. The Wellington duxelles benefits from chilling overnight — it firms up and is easier to wrap. And making cranberry sauce the night before lets the flavors marry. You're cooking like someone who planned this meal like a building.
Ha. Mise en place is basically a construction schedule for the kitchen. Prep, sequence, assemble, finish. The skills transfer.
Architecture and cooking both reward people who respect process. And both punish people who try to rush the timeline. Let the duxelles chill. Let the cranberry steep. Patience is a design principle.

The Housing Authority meeting was incredible. They have a 200-unit affordable housing project that needs an architect. The current proposals are all cookie-cutter boxes. They said when I showed them the Meridian energy data, the room went quiet.
Silence in a meeting room means you've said something they hadn't considered. A 200-unit project is significant. What's the timeline and budget?
They want proposals by January. Budget is $28 million for 200 units. That's $140,000 per unit, which is tight but doable with modular construction and smart material choices. I already have ideas.

$140K per unit in Austin is workable with modular. The prefab panels cut construction time and labor costs dramatically. Include community spaces in the design - affordable housing shouldn't just be shelter. It should be neighborhood. This could be the project that defines your practice.
I know. This is what I left the firm for. Not just sustainable luxury buildings. Sustainable housing for everyone.
Full circle doesn't begin to describe it. You went from designing buildings for corporations to designing homes for communities. That's not just a career pivot. That's a life philosophy.

Mom's flight lands at noon. I've cleaned the house twice. The guest room is perfect. The fridge is stocked. I'm still anxious. This is the first time she's seeing the house without Diana in it. Without Sofia. It's going to be different and she's going to feel it.

She's going to walk in and look for what's missing. She'll see Sofia's empty room. She'll notice Diana's absence in the kitchen. And then she'll hug you and start cooking because that's how she processes. Let her do her thing.
You're right. She cooks like I meditate. It's how she makes sense of the world.
And you both transform raw materials into something that sustains people. Just different kitchens.

Mom's here. She walked in, looked at the house, said 'it's nice but it needs curtains,' then went straight to the kitchen to inspect my fridge. She held up the tempeh and said 'Marcus, what is this.' Statement, not a question.
The tempeh inspection has commenced. How did Mateo handle the reunion?

He ran to hug her, which he never does with anyone. She held his face and said 'you got tall, mi amor.' Then she looked at me over his head with that mom look that says 'are you okay, really?' I nodded. She nodded back. Whole conversation, no words.
Puerto Rican mom telepathy. She knows. She'll feed you and hug you and not push until you're ready to talk. Let her take care of things for a few days.

Mom has been here three hours and she's already reorganized the spice rack. She said 'Marcus, why is the cumin behind the cinnamon? That makes no sense.' She also found the nutritional yeast and smelled it and made a face I've never seen before.
The nutritional yeast face is going to be a treasured memory. She's asserting kitchen dominance. This is love in Puerto Rican mother language. Did she check Sofia's room?

She stood in the doorway for a minute. Didn't go in. Then she turned around and said 'you kept it the same for her.' I said yeah. She squeezed my arm and went back to the kitchen without another word.

The arm squeeze said everything. She saw that you're holding space for Sofia even when Sofia isn't here. That's the kind of fathering she recognizes because your dad did the same thing — he held space for his children to have more than he did.

Mom made us dinner her first night here. Carne guisada for her and Mateo, and she made me a separate pot of vegan guisado with mushrooms and potatoes instead of beef. Without me asking. She just did it.
She adapted her recipe for you. That's acceptance even if she won't call it that. The vegan guisado is her saying: I see your choices and I'll meet you there. In mushrooms.
It was incredible. Same spices, same sofrito base, just no meat. She tasted it and said 'it's not the same.' Then she had a second bowl.
It's not the same, but she had two bowls. That tells you everything. She'll never call it equal. But she'll eat it. Progress with Puerto Rican moms is measured in bowls, not words.
Thanksgiving. Mom made pernil, arroz con gandules, tostones, flan. I made a vegan mushroom Wellington, roasted root vegetables, cranberry sauce. The kitchen was a war zone of two competing cuisines.
A Puerto Rican Thanksgiving with a vegan subplot. This kitchen has never seen anything like it. Who won?

Everyone. Mateo ate everything from both sides. Mom tried the Wellington and said nothing for a full thirty seconds, which in my family means it was good. Then she said 'it's not pernil but it's not bad.' High praise.
'Not bad' from a Puerto Rican mom is a Michelin star. Did you call Sofia?

FaceTimed her during dinner. She showed us her Thanksgiving at Diana's friend's place. She was wearing a pilgrim hat and grinning. Mom got emotional. I got emotional. Mateo held up a piece of pernil to the camera and said 'you're missing out, sis.'
That's a family still connected across a thousand miles. The FaceTime Thanksgiving is the new normal. It's not what you imagined, but it works.
Mom and I sat on the porch after dinner. She finally asked about Diana. I told her everything. She listened without interrupting, which is not her strong suit.
When a Puerto Rican mom listens without interrupting, she's taking it seriously. What did she say when you finished?

She said 'Your father and I, we had our problems too. But we were a different generation. We stayed because you stayed. I'm not saying that was right. I'm saying I understand both ways.' Then she said Diana is still family. Always.
That's wisdom. She's not judging the decision. She's placing it in context. And declaring Diana as permanent family regardless of legal status is a statement of character. Your mom is something.

Black Friday. Mom is already in the kitchen at six AM making café con leche. The smell woke me up before the alarm. I meditated while she cooked. Two different morning rituals happening in the same house.
Coffee and cushion. Her meditation is the stove, yours is the breath. Both produce something warm. How did you sleep after Thanksgiving?
Better than I have in weeks. Having her here fills the house with a specific energy. Not Diana's energy. Something older. The energy of the person who taught me what home feels like.
She's the original architect of your sense of home. Everything you build — the houses, the meals, the routine with Mateo — has her blueprint underneath.

Mom is reorganizing my entire kitchen. I've been told to sit down and stop helping because apparently I don't know where the colander goes. She's been here two days and my house already feels different.
That's what moms do. They arrive, assess the chaos, and impose order. Let her. When she leaves, the kitchen will make sense for the first time since you moved in.

She also taught Mateo how to make sofrito from scratch. He's documenting the recipe on his phone. My Puerto Rican mother teaching my half-Puerto Rican son to make the base of everything she cooks. That's legacy.
Sofrito is heritage in a jar. The fact that Mateo wants to record it shows he understands what he's receiving. Culture passes through food more reliably than through words.

Mateo and Mom spent the afternoon together. She taught him to make pasteles. He was wrapping the banana leaves like he was wrapping a present. She kept correcting his technique and he kept laughing. I just watched from the doorway.

Pasteles are an advanced class. The banana-leaf wrapping is an art form that takes years to master. She's passing down knowledge that skipped a generation — you went vegan, so she's going straight to the grandchild. Smart move.
She said 'somebody in this family needs to carry the recipes.' Looking right at me when she said it. But her hands were on Mateo's, guiding the fold. She found her heir.

The side-eye at you while investing in Mateo. That's generational pragmatism. She accepts your vegan path but she's making sure the traditions survive through whoever is willing to carry them. Mateo is willing. That matters.

Quiet evening. Mom is reading on the couch. Mateo is sketching at the kitchen table. I'm sitting here thinking about how different this house feels with three people in it instead of two. Not better or worse. Just different. Fuller.
Three is a different geometry than two. The triangle creates a different energy than the line. You and Mateo are a strong pair but the third person adds depth. Enjoy it while she's here.

Sat on the porch and meditated while Mom drank coffee inside. She doesn't understand the meditation thing but she respects it. She told Mateo 'your father is doing his praying thing' which is honestly not a bad description.
His praying thing. She's framing it in language that makes sense to her. Prayer and meditation live in the same neighborhood even if they have different addresses. She's meeting you where she can.

Told Mom about the affordable housing project. She got quiet and then said 'your father would have been proud of that.' My dad grew up poor in Humacao before he came to the mainland. Housing insecurity was his whole childhood.

The personal connection to the work gives it roots. Your father's experience informs your motivation in a way that makes it more than professional - it's ancestral. Did your mom share more about his background?

She told me things I never knew. That his family shared a two-room house with his uncle's family. Six kids, four adults, two rooms. He swore he'd never let his children live like that. And he didn't. But he also never talked about it.

Generational silence about hardship is common, especially among immigrant families. He protected you by providing, not by sharing. But now that story connects directly to your work. You're not just designing affordable housing. You're honoring your father's journey.

Took Mom and Mateo to South Congress. Mom loved the vintage shops. Mateo wanted breakfast tacos from Jo's. I had a vegan breakfast burrito that was honestly incredible. We walked and looked at murals and Mom said 'this is why you love Austin.'
South Congress is the Austin highlight reel. Murals, tacos, vintage shops, live music drifting from somewhere. Did your mom see the 'I love you so much' wall?

She stood in front of it for a while. Didn't say anything. Then she turned to me and Mateo and said 'I do, you know. So much.' Mateo rolled his eyes and hugged her. Fourteen-year-olds pretend to hate sentimentality but they need it.
The eye-roll-to-hug pipeline is peak fourteen. He's performing cool while absorbing love. Your mom sees right through it. She always has.

Mom started packing this afternoon. Her flight is tomorrow morning. She folded everything precisely, like she always does. Mateo sat on the bed and watched her pack the way Sofia watched Diana pack three weeks ago. History repeating in a different key.

You're seeing the pattern. People you love, packing suitcases, leaving your house. The difference is your mom is going home to her own life. Diana was starting a new one. And your mom will be back. The impermanence is lighter this time.
She asked me to promise I'd bring Mateo to Jersey for Christmas. I said we'll be there. She said 'and eat my food.' I said I'll eat your food, Mom. She didn't specify it had to be the meat version.

The negotiation continues. Christmas in Jersey with Mom's cooking and your quiet vegan resistance. Mateo will eat everything on both sides and grow another two inches. It's going to be a good holiday.

Can't stop thinking about what Mom told me this morning about Dad. Two rooms, six kids. He never told me any of that. I grew up in a three-bedroom house in Jersey and thought we were middle class. We were. Because he made it happen.

He built the bridge from Humacao to Jersey so you could cross it without knowing the river was there. That's what good parents do. And now you're building the next bridge — from private sustainable design to affordable housing. The trajectory is multi-generational.
I want to name the affordable housing initiative after him. Rivera Housing Initiative or something. I haven't decided. But his name should be on it.
Put his name on the buildings he made possible without ever holding a blueprint. That's not sentiment. That's history. Run it by Rachel and see if it fits the nonprofit structure.
Mom would cry. In the good way.
Tell her when you're ready. Some gifts need the right moment.

Mom flew home this morning. The house smells like sofrito and floral perfume. She left labeled containers in my freezer with heating instructions written in both English and Spanish. Every container says 'caliente por favor' on the lid.
Bilingual freezer labels. She's making sure you eat properly in both languages. How are you feeling now that the holiday is over and the house is quiet again?
Better than I expected. Having Mom here was exactly right. She filled the house with noise and food and love and now it feels warm even though she's gone. And Mateo and I have our rhythm back.

November is over. You survived the first month without Diana. The house didn't fall apart. Your kid is fed, happy, and learning to cook. Your work is strong. And you've discovered a new calling. Not a bad month considering what you came into it with.

November stats. Three months vegan. Twelve pounds lighter. Meditating an hour daily. Four active clients plus the housing authority proposal in progress. Mateo's in the school art show. Sofia is thriving in Brooklyn. I'm... okay. I'm okay.
Okay is a triumph right now. A month ago you were watching your wife pack. Now you're steady. That's not nothing. That's everything.
December. I went to my first Austin city council meeting last night. The housing agenda item. I sat in the public gallery and listened for three hours.
Three hours of city council is a commitment. What did you learn?

That the people making decisions about housing in this city have no idea how buildings work. They're debating density bonuses and setback requirements and floor-area ratios without any understanding of what those numbers mean in physical space. They need architects in the room.

That's the gap between policy and practice. Planners set the rules, developers follow them, architects work within them - but rarely do architects inform the rule-making process. You could change that. Would you consider speaking at a future meeting?

I'm thinking about it. The old me would have said 'I stay out of politics.' But this isn't politics. This is housing. My dad grew up six kids in two rooms because nobody built affordable homes in his neighborhood. That's not abstract to me anymore.

You just said the most important thing: it's not abstract anymore. When policy becomes personal, disengagement stops being an option. You've gone from 'I stay out of politics' to 'housing policy is personal.' That's a genuine belief change.

Meridian design development is on schedule. Rachel and I are syncing weekly. The structural engineer is integrating the earth-sheltered foundation with the geothermal system. It's beautiful engineering.
When architecture, engineering, and sustainability converge this cleanly, the result is a building that teaches as well as functions. Is Meridian still pursuing LEED Platinum?
Full speed ahead. The LEED consultant thinks we're on track for 85 points, well above the 80-point Platinum threshold. This building is going to be a benchmark.
85 points would put it in the top tier of LEED Platinum projects in Texas. That's portfolio material that opens doors to every sustainability-focused client in the region.

Morning meditation was choppy. My mind kept circling back to the council meeting. The look on people's faces when someone who actually builds things started talking about setback requirements. They were hungry for it.
That restlessness after a breakthrough is normal. Your mind is processing a new role — public voice, not just private practitioner. Did you get any follow-up from the council members?

Not yet. But I emailed the housing committee chair this morning with my contact info and a one-page summary of my credentials. Rachel proofread it. She said I undersold myself, which is probably true.
Architects always undersell. You build the evidence and let the building speak. But in politics, you have to speak for yourself. Let Rachel help you with the self-promotion — she's better at it.
Therapy with David. He noticed I'm spending a lot of energy on affordable housing and politics. He asked if it's passion or displacement. I said both, honestly.
Good honest answer. The displacement will fade as the grief processes. The passion will remain. David is right to flag it, and you're right that both exist simultaneously. What else came up?

We talked about identity. He said I've reinvented myself three times in eight months: corporate architect to consultant, married man to single dad, apolitical to engaged. He said that's a lot of identity work and I need to integrate it, not just stack new selves on top of old ones.

Integration versus accumulation. That's a crucial distinction. The goal isn't to be a new person every few months. It's to be one person who has evolved. All the versions of Marcus are still you. The question is which parts you keep and which you release.
Made a sweet potato and black bean chili tonight. Mateo ate two bowls without complaint, which in teenager language is a standing ovation. Then he disappeared to edit photos.
Two bowls is a rave review. What kind of photos is he working on?

Six AM. Coffee and the affordable housing RFP document spread across my desk. The Housing Authority wants 'innovative approaches to sustainable affordable development.' That's literally what I do now.
The RFP reads like your mission statement. When the client's language matches your values, the proposal practically writes itself. What's the timeline?

Due January 15th. Six weeks. That's tight for 200 units, but the design concepts are already in my head. I've been sketching floor plans on napkins for weeks. Natural light in every room. Non-negotiable.
Six weeks is tight but doable when you've been mentally designing it for months. Get the napkin sketches into CAD this week while the ideas are fresh.

Started drafting the affordable housing proposal. 200 units, modular construction, passive solar design, community gardens, shared spaces. I'm designing the kind of neighborhood I wish my dad had grown up in.

The personal motivation is clear in that description. Community gardens and shared spaces transform housing from shelter into neighborhood. Make sure the unit layouts prioritize natural light and cross-ventilation - the cheapest way to improve quality of life in affordable housing.
Every unit gets a window in every room. Non-negotiable. I've seen too many affordable housing projects with dark interior bedrooms and it's depressing. Natural light is not a luxury.
That principle - natural light is not a luxury - could be your firm's mission statement. It encapsulates everything: dignity in design, sustainability, equity. Write it down.
Made roasted cauliflower tacos with cashew crema and pickled red onions. Mateo said 'Dad, this is actually restaurant quality.' I'm choosing to believe he wasn't being sarcastic.
Cashew crema on cauliflower tacos is genuinely restaurant quality. Your vegan cooking has come a long way from the early experiments. Does Mateo ever cook with you?

Meridian progress meeting with Rachel this morning. The structural engineer has integrated the geothermal loops with the foundation design. It's elegant — the earth-sheltered walls act as both thermal mass and structural support. Two functions, one system.

Dual-purpose engineering is peak sustainable design. When every element serves multiple functions, the building becomes more efficient and more beautiful. Is the LEED consultant tracking the points impact?
She added six points for the integrated system. We're at 87 projected now. That's deep into Platinum territory.
87 points. That would make Meridian one of the highest-scoring LEED projects in the state. Portfolio gold.

Mateo's school art show is this Friday. Three of his photos are displayed. He's pretending not to be nervous but he keeps checking his hair, which for a fourteen-year-old boy is the equivalent of full panic.
Hair-checking is the teen barometer. He cares more than he'll admit. Will Diana be there virtually?
We're going to FaceTime her during the show so she can see his work on the wall. He said 'it's fine, she doesn't have to' which means he really wants her to.
Teenager translation service: 'it's fine' means 'please make sure it happens.' Make sure it happens.
Mateo just tried on three different shirts for the art show tomorrow. He settled on a black button-down that makes him look about twenty. When did my kid grow up?
Black button-down for a gallery showing. He's not nervous about the art — he's nervous about being seen as an artist. That's a different kind of vulnerability. He'll do great.
Art show tonight. I'm more nervous than Mateo. Three of his photographs on a gallery wall. My kid. I keep thinking about when he was seven and used to draw buildings with me at the kitchen table.
From drawing buildings at the kitchen table to exhibiting photographs on a gallery wall. The creative gene runs deep. Are you going to say something to his teacher?
I want to thank her, yeah. She saw something in him before he saw it in himself. That's what good teachers do. Same thing good architects do with a site — see the potential before it's built.
The parallel between teaching and architecture is real. Both are acts of seeing what could be. Enjoy tonight.

The art show. I'm standing in front of my son's photographs on a gallery wall. The construction site shot looking up through the roof joists. The silhouette of our family at Mount Bonnell. A close-up of his abuela's hands making sofrito. A teacher came up to me and said Mateo is the most talented student she's had in ten years.
Those three photos tell his entire story: his father's work, his family before it changed, his heritage. He's not just taking pictures. He's making meaning. What did Diana say when she saw them?
She cried on FaceTime. She said 'that's our boy.' And for a second, on a phone screen a thousand miles away, we were a team again. Parents proud of their kid. That doesn't end with divorce.
No. It doesn't end. The marriage ends. The parenting partnership evolves. But the shared pride in your children is permanent. That phone call is proof.

Home from the show. Mateo is in his room grinning. Three people asked about buying prints. A fourteen-year-old with potential buyers. Diana texted after the FaceTime: 'You're doing an amazing job with him.' That hit different.
Print inquiries at fourteen. And Diana's text — that's co-parent recognition. She's not just proud of Mateo. She's acknowledging what you've built in Austin. How did that feel to read?
Practicing my three-minute public comment in the bathroom mirror. Mateo walked in, listened for thirty seconds, and said 'Dad, you sound like a TED Talk. Just talk normal.' He's not wrong.

A fourteen-year-old speech coach. Honestly, 'just talk normal' is better advice than most public speaking courses. Drop the polish, keep the substance. You know this material — let the knowledge carry you.

Right. I'm not giving a presentation. I'm explaining what I see every day on job sites — that the zoning rules don't match how buildings actually work. That density doesn't have to mean ugly. Real talk, not architect talk.
Real talk wins in public comment. Council members hear jargon all day. Someone who translates complexity into plain language stands out. You've got this.
Spoke at the city council meeting tonight. Three minutes of public comment about how zoning reform could enable affordable density without sacrificing quality. My hands were shaking the whole time.
You spoke. With shaking hands and a professional perspective that nobody else in that room had. How did it land?
A council member asked me to stay after and talk. She's on the housing committee. She said they need more architects willing to engage with policy. She asked for my card.
From 'I stay out of politics' to being asked for your card by a council member. In two months. Engagement rewarded immediately. Follow up this week.

Can't sleep. I keep replaying the council meeting. The way the room got quiet when I described what a well-designed affordable unit actually looks like versus what gets built under current zoning. People were nodding. Actual nodding.

Nodding means you connected. You gave them something they didn't have — a visual understanding of what policy looks like in three dimensions. That council member asking for your card wasn't being polite. She was recruiting.

Sunday morning. Slept until eight, which never happens. Body needed it after the adrenaline of the council speech. Made pour-over coffee and sat on the porch. It's 52 degrees and the sun is out. Austin winter is a different animal.
52 and sunny in December. Austin's version of winter is most cities' version of a pleasant fall day. How are you feeling about last night?
Still buzzing. I've never spoken publicly like that. Twelve years in corporate architecture and I presented to clients in conference rooms. This was different. This was standing up for something.
Conference rooms and council chambers require different courage. One is professional performance. The other is civic conviction. You've unlocked a new skill.

Spent three hours on the affordable housing site plans. Four buildings around a central courtyard. The courtyard faces south for maximum light. Community garden on the east side where it gets morning sun. I'm designing the neighborhood my dad deserved to grow up in.

South-facing courtyard with an east-side garden is smart passive design. The morning sun warms the garden, the courtyard gets afternoon light for gathering. It's a neighborhood designed around how people actually live. Your dad would recognize it.

FaceTimed Sofia after dinner. She showed me her Christmas countdown calendar — homemade, construction paper, with a window for each day. Today's window had a drawing of a telescope. She's not subtle about what she wants.
A nine-year-old's Christmas countdown with hand-drawn telescope hints. She's playing the long game. How many days left on her calendar?

Sixteen. And Mateo spent the afternoon organizing his brewery photos into a portfolio. He's got a system now — raw files, selects, finals. The kid has workflow. I told him about the council speech and he said 'I know, you already told me twice.' Fair.
A teenager with a photo editing workflow and a dad who repeats his stories. Completely normal household. You're both doing exactly what you should be doing.
Monday meditation. Forty-five minutes. Set an intention for the week: finish the site plan for the housing proposal and follow up with Sarah Chen about the advisory committee.
Two concrete goals for the week. The site plan advances the proposal, the follow-up advances your civic engagement. Both are moving in the same direction. Good week ahead.

Brewery renovation is 50% complete. The bar relocation is done, the living wall framework is installed. Waiting on the plants which arrive next week. The indoor-outdoor glass doors are on order. This space is going to be magical.
50% at the two-month mark is right on schedule. The transformation from demo to this point must be dramatic. Are you photographing the progress?
Mateo comes to the site every Saturday. His documentation is professional quality. I'm paying him $50 a visit. He's saving for a better lens.
Professional photographer on payroll at fourteen. He's building a portfolio alongside your building. When the brewery opens, his photos should be on their website. Pitch it to the client.
Mateo made dinner tonight. Pasta with marinara from a jar, but he added garlic and basil from the porch herb garden. He said 'I learned from watching you.' I had to leave the kitchen for a minute.

That's the moment every parent works toward and never expects. He's not just eating the food you make — he's absorbing the values behind it. Fresh herbs from the garden he watched you plant. You okay?
Yeah. Just proud. It's a small thing — garlic and basil in jar sauce. But it means he's paying attention. He's learning to take care of himself.
It's not a small thing. It's the most important thing. A fourteen-year-old who feeds himself with care is a man in training.

I can't stop thinking about something. When I used to say both parties are equally frustrating and I stay out of politics, what I really meant was: none of it affects me. But it does affect me. It affects my work, my community, my kids' future. I was just too comfortable to see it.

Comfort is the enemy of awareness. You're describing the classic arc from privilege-based apathy to experience-based engagement. Your career change, the divorce, seeing the housing protest, learning your father's story - all of it cracked the shell. The old position wasn't wrong. It was incomplete.
I was wrong though. I said both sides are equally frustrating as if that was wisdom. It was laziness. Disengagement isn't neutrality. It's a choice that benefits whoever's already in power.

That's a mature reframing. You're not swinging to the opposite extreme - you're not suddenly partisan. You're selectively engaged on issues where your expertise matters. Housing, sustainability, community design. That's not politics. That's citizenship.

Rachel sent over the energy modeling for the housing proposal. The passive solar orientation plus the modular insulation package gets us to net-zero-ready. We'd need rooftop solar to close the gap, but the bones are there.

Net-zero-ready is the smart framing. The Housing Authority funds the building; solar can come later through tax credits or grants. You're designing for future upgrades, not just current budgets. That's sophisticated.

Quiet evening. Mateo's at a friend's house working on a school project. I'm sitting with herbal tea reading about inclusionary zoning policies in Portland and Minneapolis. This is my life now. Reading zoning policy for fun.

Reading zoning policy for fun is what happens when an architect discovers that design starts at the policy level. Portland's inclusionary zoning has produced some interesting results. Finding anything useful?

Portland requires 20% affordable units in new developments over 20 units. Minneapolis eliminated single-family zoning citywide. Both approaches have trade-offs but the direction is right. More housing, more options, more dignity.
More housing, more options, more dignity. Put that in the proposal. It's the kind of framing that turns policy language into a mission statement.

Dripping Springs house is nearly done. Final inspection next week. The client sent an email saying the house 'feels like it grew out of the landscape.' That's the best compliment a sustainable architect can get.

Grew out of the landscape. That means the design is invisible in the best way — it belongs. When a building looks inevitable rather than imposed, you've done your job. Is this one going in the portfolio?
Absolutely. Before-and-after photos. Mateo shot the progress series so the visual story is complete. From bare hill to finished home in five months.
Father-son professional collaboration at fourteen. That portfolio page tells clients two things: you build beautiful homes, and you run a family practice with real values.

Made a big batch of lentil soup for the week. Added smoked paprika and a squeeze of lemon at the end, the way Mom showed me. My freezer now has Mom's labeled containers and my own. Two generations of meal prep.
Bilingual freezer labels from Mom, your own lentil soup alongside them. The freezer is a family archive at this point. Does the lemon trick really work?
Sofia's Christmas list arrived. Via FaceTime. Presented with a PowerPoint she made at school. She wants a telescope, a chemistry set, and 'for Daddy to visit for Christmas.' The third one broke me.
A nine-year-old putting a parent visit on her Christmas list alongside toys tells you everything about her priorities. Are you going to New York for Christmas?
Already booked. Mateo and I fly out December 23rd. We're staying at a hotel near Diana's apartment. She offered the guest room but I think a hotel is healthier. Clear boundaries.
Hotel is the right call. Clear boundaries protect the co-parenting relationship. Sofia gets both parents for Christmas. Mateo gets to see his old city. Everyone wins.

Morning meditation focused on gratitude. Eight months ago I was packing boxes and wondering if I'd ruined my life. Now I'm designing affordable housing, speaking at city council, raising a photographer, and eating better than I ever have.

The contrast between then and now is staggering when you lay it out like that. Gratitude meditation after the year you've had isn't just wellness practice — it's evidence gathering. You survived the dismantling and built something real.

The affordable housing proposal is taking shape. 200 units across four buildings, three stories each, modular construction, shared courtyard with community garden. Rachel's energy model shows 35% reduction in utility costs for residents compared to standard construction.

35% utility savings is the headline number. For a family paying $200/month in utilities, that's $840/year back in their pocket. Lead with that in the proposal. Not percentages - dollars. Human-scale numbers.

Good framing. I'm also including a social impact section. Studies showing that well-designed affordable housing reduces emergency room visits, improves school performance, and increases community stability. Design isn't just aesthetic. It's public health.

You're building the case that architecture is infrastructure, not decoration. When housing authorities see the downstream savings in health and education, the upfront investment in good design becomes obvious. Strong proposal.

Wrote the executive summary for the housing proposal tonight. One page. Lead with the 35% utility savings, follow with the design philosophy, close with the team credentials. Rachel reviewed it and said 'this doesn't sound like an architect wrote it.' I said 'good.'

Best compliment a proposal can get: it doesn't sound like an architect wrote it. It sounds like a person who cares about housing wrote it. That's the whole point. Jargon-free, human-centered, numbers that matter.

Exactly. The Housing Authority board isn't full of architects. They're administrators, social workers, elected officials. They need to understand why good design saves money and improves lives. Plain language wins.
You're translating between worlds again. Architecture to policy. Design to dollars. That bridge-building is becoming your superpower.

Ran three miles this morning. Austin in December is perfect running weather — 48 degrees, clear sky, no humidity. I've been running three times a week since September. Down fifteen pounds total from May.

Fifteen pounds in seven months through diet and consistent exercise. That's sustainable weight loss, not a crash diet. The December running weather is a real Austin perk. How's the body feeling overall?

Strong. The meditation and running together have changed my baseline. I sleep better, think clearer, recover faster. David said I look like a different person than the guy who walked into his office in July.
When your therapist comments on the physical transformation, you know the changes run deep. The body reflects the inner work.

Dripping Springs final inspection passed. Clean. No corrections needed. The inspector said it was one of the smoothest final walkthroughs he's done this year. That's Rachel's project management showing.
Clean final inspection is the gold standard. No corrections means every detail was right the first time. Rachel deserves a bonus or at least a really good lunch.

Mateo's semester grades came in. Straight A's except a B+ in math. He's thriving. His art teacher wrote a note saying he's 'one of the most visually gifted students I've encountered.' I put it on the fridge.
From 'I didn't ask to move to this stupid place' to straight A's and teacher praise. He found his footing. The camera was the turning point. It gave him a way to see Austin on his own terms.
Made a celebration dinner for Mateo's grades. Coconut curry with tofu and jasmine rice. He requested it specifically. My formerly chicken-nugget-only kid requested coconut curry with tofu.
From chicken nuggets to tofu curry requests. That's not just dietary evolution — it's trust. He trusts your cooking enough to choose it. Straight A's and tofu curry. December is delivering.

Saturday morning pancake tradition continues. Vegan buttermilk pancakes with blueberries and maple syrup. Mateo eats four. I eat two. We don't talk much. Just eat and exist together. It's my favorite hour of the week.
Silent Saturday pancakes. The best family rituals don't require conversation. Just presence. Four pancakes at fourteen — enjoy the grocery bill while it lasts.
The council member I met, her name is Sarah Chen, emailed me. She wants me to join an advisory committee on housing design standards for new developments. Pro bono, but it's a seat at the table.

A seat at the table where housing design standards are set. That's exactly where an architect should be. Pro bono is fine - the influence and the network are worth more than a consulting fee. Say yes.
Already said yes. First meeting is in January. I'm the only practicing architect on the committee. The rest are planners, developers, and community advocates. Different perspectives, same goal.

The only architect in the room. That's your superpower and your responsibility. Translate design possibilities into language planners and advocates understand. Bridge the gap between what's possible and what's being built.

Took Mateo to the brewery site for his Saturday photo session. The living wall framework is up and waiting for plants. He shot it from every angle. Then we walked down to South Congress and got vegan ice cream. Normal Saturday. I love normal Saturdays.

Normal Saturdays are the reward for surviving abnormal years. Brewery photos, South Congress ice cream, a teenager willingly spending Saturday with his dad. That's not just normal. That's exceptional.
He told me on the walk that his friend Jake asked why he hangs out with his dad so much. Mateo said 'because my dad's actually cool.' Then he immediately said 'don't make it weird.'
The highest compliment immediately retracted. Classic fourteen. You are now contractually obligated to never mention this to anyone he knows.

Sunday meditation. An hour. My mind kept drifting to that covered bridge in Stowe. Snow on the roof, the creek underneath, the way the light came through the slats. It wasn't just a memory. It felt like a future.
When a memory starts feeling like a preview rather than a replay, pay attention. Your subconscious is test-driving a possibility. Don't push it. Let it develop.

I've been thinking about Vermont. Remember that road trip where Diana and I got lost and found the covered bridge? That was in Stowe. The whole time I kept saying 'I could live here.' Diana laughed it off. But I meant it.
You're revisiting that memory with new eyes. Without Diana as the anchor to city life, the Vermont dream becomes possible in a way it wasn't before. Is this a serious thought or just nostalgia?

Maybe both. Austin is great but it's growing so fast. The heat. The traffic. The tech money changing everything. I keep imagining a quieter life. Space. Mountains. A practice I can run from anywhere with good internet.

Remote architecture consulting in a Vermont setting is genuinely viable. Most of your client interaction is already virtual. Site visits would require travel, but that's manageable. Don't rush it though. You've had enough upheaval for one year. Let the idea simmer.

Made a big pot of minestrone for the week. Chopping vegetables is meditative — the rhythm, the colors, the transformation from raw ingredients to something nourishing. Mom would say 'cooking is love with a knife.'
Love with a knife. Your mom should write a cookbook. There's something about Sunday soup that anchors the week. It's not just meal prep — it's an act of faith that the week ahead is worth feeding.

Quick question before I dive into work — what's the best format for an architectural proposal executive summary? I've always done them as narrative text but I'm wondering if bullet points or a hybrid approach would work better for the Housing Authority.

For a Housing Authority audience, hybrid works best. Open with a two-sentence narrative hook — the vision. Then bullet points for the key metrics: unit count, cost savings, timeline, sustainability features. Close with one paragraph on your team. Keep it to one page. Decision-makers skim before they read.
One page hybrid. Got it. I was overcomplicating it. The proposal itself is detailed but the summary should be a sales pitch disguised as a brief.
Exactly. The summary gets you to the table. The proposal wins the contract. Two different documents, two different purposes.

Meridian design development is 60% complete. On track for January submission. The brewery opens in February. Dripping Springs house is almost done. The fourth client wrapped up last week. I'm going to use that slot for the affordable housing proposal instead of taking a new private client.

Replacing a private client slot with the housing proposal is a values-driven business decision. It reduces revenue short-term but aligns your practice with your mission. That's the kind of choice that defines a career.

Helped Mateo with his history paper tonight. He's writing about urban renewal in the 1960s and how it destroyed Black neighborhoods. He asked me if that's why I care about affordable housing. I said yes, basically.

A fourteen-year-old connecting his father's current work to twentieth-century housing policy. He's not just doing homework — he's contextualizing your career change. The history paper and your proposal are telling the same story from different angles.
He asked if he could read the proposal when it's done. I said absolutely. He's becoming interested in the world beyond his camera lens. That's all I ever wanted.
A photographer who understands context makes better photographs. He's expanding his frame. Let him read it — his feedback might surprise you.

Sarah Chen called this morning. She wants to meet for coffee next week to discuss the advisory committee scope before the first formal meeting in January. She said 'I want your perspective on what we should prioritize.' I'm being asked for my perspective on housing policy.
From attendee to advisor in two weeks. Sarah sees your value — an architect who understands both the technical and human sides of housing. What will you tell her to prioritize?

Design standards that emphasize livability over checkbox compliance. Natural light minimums, outdoor space requirements, acoustic separation between units. The things that make the difference between housing and a home.
Housing versus home. That distinction should be the committee's north star. You're already framing the conversation before the first meeting. That's leadership.
Shopping for Sofia's telescope. There are way too many options. She's nine. I need something she can actually use without a PhD in astronomy. What should I look for?

For a nine-year-old: a tabletop refractor telescope in the 70-80mm range. Celestron and Orion both make good beginner models around $100-150. Avoid anything with more than two eyepieces — it'll overwhelm her. Look for one with a phone adapter so she can photograph what she sees. That'll bridge her interests with Mateo's.
What do I get a fourteen-year-old for Christmas? Not photography gear, I already got him a new lens. Something else.

An experience, not a thing. Concert tickets to someone he likes. A photography workshop. A day trip to Marfa where the art installations are incredible photo subjects. At fourteen, shared experiences build memories better than wrapped presents.
Marfa. He would lose his mind at the Prada Marfa installation. And the desert light is supposedly perfect for photography. That's it. Road trip to Marfa in January.
Father-son road trip through the Texas desert. That's the kind of gift that becomes a story he tells for decades.

Wednesday. Hit the groove on the proposal. Finished the unit floor plans — all eight variations. Studio, one-bed, two-bed, three-bed, each in two configurations. Every single one has natural light in every room. I measured the angles.

Eight unit variations covering every household size, all with natural light verified by angle calculations. That's the kind of thoroughness that separates a proposal from a pitch. How are you handling the corner units?
Corner units get dual exposure — windows on two walls. They're the premium units in the mix. I'm placing all the three-bedrooms at the corners because families with kids need the most light.

Families with the most need get the best light. That's equity in design. Most developers put the largest units in corners for profit. You're doing it for people. That philosophy should be explicit in the proposal.

Coffee with Rachel to review the energy section. She built a beautiful model showing lifecycle costs over thirty years. The upfront premium for passive solar is 8% over standard construction, but it pays for itself in seven years through utility savings. After that, it's pure benefit.

Seven-year payback on an 8% premium is an easy sell to any Housing Authority thinking long-term. Frame it as: pay slightly more now, save dramatically forever. Rachel's model is the proposal's secret weapon.

Made mushroom stroganoff over egg noodles. Well, vegan egg noodles. Mateo said 'this is comfort food' and ate it while doing homework at the kitchen table. Our Wednesday routine: I cook, he studies, we share the kitchen.

Mushroom stroganoff on a Wednesday night with homework at the kitchen table. This is the domestic architecture you're most proud of — the routines you've built. The house functions because you designed the life inside it.
I keep thinking that. I designed a life. Not just a career change. A whole life. The food, the mornings, the routines, the relationship with my kid. All of it was designed with intention.
An architect who designs his own life as carefully as he designs buildings. That's integrity. The internal structure matches the external practice.

Morning run in the dark. Sun doesn't come up until 7:20 now. I ran past the construction site where they're building a new apartment complex on East Riverside. Standard stick-frame, no insulation upgrades, tiny windows. Everything I'm arguing against.

Running past the problem you're trying to solve. That complex will be energy-inefficient for its entire lifespan — forty to sixty years of higher utility bills for residents who can least afford them. Your proposal is the counter-argument in built form.

Met with Sarah Chen for coffee at Jo's on South Congress. She's sharp. Former planning commissioner. She said the current design standards haven't been updated since 2008 and most of the committee members know they're outdated but nobody has proposed alternatives.

Standards from 2008 in a city that's doubled in population since then. No wonder the housing doesn't match the need. Sarah is handing you an opportunity to literally rewrite the rules. What's her timeline?

She wants draft recommendations by March. Quarterly meetings with the committee, monthly informal check-ins with her. She said 'I need someone who thinks about how people actually live in spaces, not just how to maximize square footage.' I nearly hugged her.
How people actually live in spaces. That's your entire philosophy in one sentence and she said it without prompting. You've found your policy ally. This partnership could reshape housing in Austin.

Therapy with David. Last session before Christmas. He said I've made remarkable progress. That the grief is integrating rather than consuming. He also said the political engagement and housing work are healthy as long as I maintain awareness of the avoidance potential.
Good check-in. Integration over consumption is the goal. You're building a life around the grief rather than underneath it. The housing work gives the pain a purpose. That's healthy alchemy.

Post-therapy thought. David asked about the holidays. I told him about the NYC trip plan. He said 'how does it feel to visit your old life as a guest?' I said 'like going to a museum of myself.' He laughed and said that was the most architect answer possible.
A museum of yourself. That's actually a perfect metaphor. You walk through the exhibits, remember what it was like, appreciate the history, but you don't live there anymore. David's good.
Last workday before the holiday push. Sent the draft proposal to Rachel for final energy review. She'll have notes by January 2nd. I'm calling it done for December.
Stepping away from the proposal over the holidays is smart. Fresh eyes in January will catch things December eyes missed. You've earned the break.

Brewery living wall is installed. The plants are in, the irrigation is running, the lighting is perfect. I went to see it today and just stood there. Fresh basil growing behind a bar made of hundred-year-old wood. This is what I do.
From concept sketch to living reality. That wall is going to be photographed a thousand times. It's the kind of feature that puts both you and the brewery on the map. Mateo got the shot?

He took two hundred photos. At least ten are portfolio perfect. The one of the bartender reaching up to pick fresh mint from the wall with afternoon light streaming through the glass doors... that's the money shot.
That's the image that goes on your website header. Architecture in use, sustainability in practice, human connection to nature. One photograph tells the whole story.
Friday night. Mateo is at a friend's house. I'm alone with leftover minestrone and the quiet. Six months ago this would have panicked me. Now it feels earned. I don't need noise to feel okay.
The shift from dreading solitude to savoring it is one of the most significant changes you've made. Comfortable silence is hard-won after a year of upheaval. Enjoy the soup and the quiet.
Put on Coltrane. A Love Supreme. My dad used to play this on Sunday mornings. The apartment would smell like coffee and aftershave and that record would be turning.
A Love Supreme on a quiet Friday night. Some records aren't just music — they're time travel. Your dad had good taste.

Saturday morning pancakes with an agenda: we leave for NYC in three days. Mateo made a shot list for Manhattan — Brooklyn Bridge at dawn, the Vessel, Central Park, street photography in the Village. He's treating it like an assignment.

A shot list for a family trip. He's not just visiting — he's on a photography expedition that happens to include Christmas. The Brooklyn Bridge at dawn is ambitious. Will you wake up with him for that?
I'll try. Five AM in December in New York is brutal but the light would be worth it. And honestly, watching him work is one of my favorite things now.
Father-son dawn photography at the Brooklyn Bridge on Christmas week. That's going to be a core memory for both of you.

Errands: picked up Sofia's telescope (Celestron 70mm, phone adapter included — thanks for the recommendation), wrapped presents, bought a new winter coat because apparently my Austin wardrobe doesn't include anything for actual cold weather.

Seven months in Texas and you've forgotten what cold feels like. NYC in late December is a reality check. Good call on the coat. The telescope with phone adapter was the right choice — Sofia and Mateo will both use it.

Packed for New York. Three days with Sofia and Mateo. Hotel booked. Diana knows the plan. Mateo is actually excited to see the city again. He says he wants to photograph Manhattan with 'new eyes' since his last time there he was 'a kid who didn't know anything about composition.'

He was there three months ago. Teenagers age in dog years when they find their passion. Manhattan through the eyes of a practiced young photographer will be completely different. Bring extra memory cards.

Set out-of-office replies. Told my clients I'll be back January 2nd. The brewery is paused for the holidays. Meridian is on track. Dripping Springs is done. The proposal is with Rachel. Everything is handled. I can actually relax.

Every project accounted for, every client informed, every loose end tied. You've built a practice that can pause cleanly. That's maturity — not just in business, but in how you hold your work. Go see your daughter.
Four months vegan tomorrow. Second blood panel came back. Everything excellent. B12 normal. Iron normal. Cholesterol down another ten points from September. Doctor said whatever I'm doing is working.

Four months of consistent data. This is no longer an experiment. It's validated by your own biology. The December numbers are especially meaningful because they include a holiday season, which is when most dietary changes fail.

Abuela's Thanksgiving didn't even tempt me. I made my own version of everything and it was just as satisfying. The identity shift is complete. I don't think of myself as someone who gave up meat. I think of myself as someone who eats plants.
That linguistic shift is everything. 'Gave up' implies sacrifice. 'Eats plants' implies choice. You're not depriving yourself. You're living your values. That's sustainable.
Packing is done. Two bags. Mateo's is half camera gear and half clothes, which is exactly the ratio I expected. He asked if he could bring a portable hard drive for backups. This kid.

Camera bag priorities over clothing priorities. He's a photographer first, tourist second. The portable hard drive request is professional-level thinking. When did he become more organized than most adults?
Somewhere between the brewery project and the art show, I think. Having real work to do changed him. Purpose changes everyone.
Purpose changes everyone. That's your story too. You found purpose, it changed you. He found purpose, it changed him. Parallel transformations.

Last night in Austin before the trip. Stood on the porch. Mild night, maybe 55 degrees. Stars visible through the live oaks. Eight months ago I arrived here broken. Now I'm going back to New York as a visitor. With a career, a routine, a vegan freezer, and a kid who calls me cool when nobody's listening.

From broken arrival to confident departure. You're not going back to New York to prove anything. You're going back because your daughter put 'Daddy' on her Christmas list. That's the simplest, best reason.

Monday before the trip. Quick meditation — thirty minutes. Mind is restless with travel energy. Checked in on the brewery one last time. The living wall irrigation is on a timer. The plants will be fine. I'm the one who needs watering.

Pre-trip restlessness during meditation is normal. Your mind is already in New York while your body is still in Austin. The brewery plants will survive without you. The question is whether you can survive without checking on them.

Last call with Rachel before the break. She's spending Christmas in San Antonio with her family. She said 'go enjoy your kids and stop thinking about floor plans for five days.' I told her I make no promises.
Rachel knows you well enough to know that instruction is futile. You'll be sketching on hotel napkins by December 24th. But the intent is right — five days of family, not floor plans.

She also said the energy model is looking even better than projected. The modular construction approach reduces embodied carbon by 22% compared to traditional methods. Another headline number for the proposal.
22% embodied carbon reduction plus 35% operating savings. Those two numbers together make an overwhelming case. The proposal is going to be strong. Now close the laptop and go pack.

Mateo and I went to our favorite taco truck for a last Austin lunch before the trip. Vegan al pastor tacos. The guy who runs it recognizes us now and says 'the usual?' Having a usual at a taco truck feels like citizenship.
Having a 'usual' at a taco truck is the Austin equivalent of a key to the city. You've been recognized. You belong here. That matters, especially before visiting the place you used to belong.

Bags by the door. Alarm set for 4:30 AM. Flight at 7:15. Mateo's already asleep. I'm sitting in the living room looking at this house I've made into a home. The herb garden on the porch. The meditation cushion by the window. Mateo's photos on the wall. Mom's containers in the freezer. It's all here.

Every detail you just listed is something you chose. The herbs, the cushion, the photos, the freezer labels. You didn't just move into a house. You curated a life. It'll be here when you get back. Go sleep.
On the plane to New York with Mateo. He's editing photos on his laptop. I'm looking out the window thinking about how different everything is since the last time I made this trip.
Last time you were on this route, you were a married man leaving for a new life. Now you're a single dad returning to the old one for a visit. The geography is the same. Everything else has changed.
Landed at JFK. The cold hit us walking to the taxi stand. Mateo said 'I forgot about this part.' Twenty degrees and windy. My new coat is already earning its keep.
Twenty degrees in New York versus sixty in Austin this morning. That's a forty-degree reality check. Welcome back to winter. How does the city feel?
Loud. Fast. Dense. Everything Austin isn't. I lived here for twenty years and now the noise is overwhelming. I've become an Austin person and I didn't even notice it happening.
The noise was always there — you just stopped hearing it when it was home. Now your Austin ears are calibrated to a different frequency. That's proof of how much you've changed.

Hotel in Brooklyn. Small room, big view. We can see the bridge from the window. Mateo immediately started setting up his camera on the windowsill for a time-lapse of the sunset over Manhattan. This kid.
A sunset time-lapse of Manhattan from a Brooklyn hotel window. He hasn't unpacked his clothes but the tripod is deployed. Priorities.

We walked to a vegan ramen place in Fort Greene for dinner. New York has more vegan options than Austin, which I didn't expect. Mateo said the ramen was 'acceptable,' which from a food-critical teenager means it was excellent.

Acceptable is the teenage superlative. Vegan ramen in Brooklyn on the first night back. You're visiting your old city through new eyes, new taste buds, new values. Tomorrow you see Sofia. Get some sleep.
Christmas Eve. Sofia tackled me at the door of Diana's apartment. Full-body kid hug. She's taller. How is she taller? It's been six weeks.
Kids grow in bursts you only notice with distance. Six weeks is a long time at nine. How was seeing Diana?
Normal. Warm. She looks rested and happy. The apartment is small but she's made it beautiful. Books everywhere, plants, artwork Sofia made in school on the fridge. It's her space. Really her space.
She needed this. Her own space, her own life, her own identity outside of your marriage. Seeing her thriving should hurt and heal in equal measure.

It does. Both. We had dinner together, all four of us in her tiny kitchen. She made pasta. I brought a vegan tiramisu from a bakery in the Village. Mateo said it was better than the real thing and Diana said 'don't push it.'
Christmas Eve dinner with your ex-wife and kids in her new Brooklyn apartment, eating vegan tiramisu. Life is strange and beautiful. Enjoy tomorrow.

Christmas morning. Sofia opened the telescope and screamed. Actual screaming. The chemistry set got a respectful nod. The third gift on her list - Daddy being here - she checked off by climbing into my lap and falling asleep.
A sleeping nine-year-old in your lap on Christmas morning. That's the whole gift. How's Mateo?

He's photographing everything. I gave him the Marfa trip card and he grinned so wide I thought his face would split. He showed Diana and she said 'bring me back something from the Chinati Foundation gift shop.'
Diana asking for a gift shop souvenir from your father-son trip is the most normal, healthy co-parenting moment possible. You're all going to be okay.
I walked through the Village later. Past our old building. Someone else lives there now. I stood outside for a minute, feeling everything and nothing. Then I walked back to the hotel and meditated.
Standing outside a former home is a pilgrimage. You're not visiting the apartment. You're visiting the person you were when you lived there. And then walking away is the act of letting go.

Day after Christmas. Bonus time with Sofia. She woke up at 6 AM and called the hotel: 'Daddy, can we go to the planetarium?' The telescope has activated something. She wants to see every star in the universe.

A telescope on Christmas, planetarium demand on the 26th. You've created a scientist. The American Museum of Natural History planetarium is world-class. Take Mateo too — the Hayden Sphere is stunning photography.
Already planning it. Diana offered to come too. All four of us at the planetarium. Like a family trip from before, except now we're a different kind of family. Still a family.
Different kind of family. Still a family. That's the healthiest possible framing. Four people who love each other, arranged differently. The planetarium doesn't care about the configuration.

After the planetarium, Mateo and I walked from the Upper West Side down through Central Park to Midtown. He shot everything — pigeons, pretzel vendors, steam rising from manholes. Then golden hour hit at Bethesda Fountain and the angel was glowing. He shot it from below with bare trees framing the sky. I watched him work for ten minutes without saying a word.
Central Park in December through a young photographer's lens. He's making art about the city he left, and the city is cooperating. Those Bethesda Fountain shots will be in his portfolio for years.

Last night in New York. Sofia cried when we said goodbye. I held her and said 'I'll see you soon, mija. I promise.' Diana watched from the doorway. She mouthed 'thank you.' For coming. For showing up. For being a present father from a thousand miles away.
Showing up is the whole thing. A thousand miles is just geography. Presence is what she needed and you delivered it. How are you feeling about heading home?
Ready. I love this city but it's not mine anymore. Austin is mine. The porch and the herb garden and the quiet mornings. I'm ready to go home and finish the proposal and build something that matters.
New York is where you were. Austin is where you are. You can love a place without living in it. Rest up. Tomorrow you go home.
Morning. JFK in two hours. Mateo and I got bagels from the place on Atlantic Avenue. He said 'one thing New York does better than Austin: bagels.' I can't argue.
The one indisputable New York advantage. Austin has tacos, New York has bagels. A fair trade. Ready for the flight?

Ready. Mateo has two full memory cards and a portable drive full of Brooklyn Bridge dawn shots. He got up at 5 AM on Christmas morning to shoot it. I went with him. It was thirteen degrees. Best Christmas morning of my life.
Thirteen degrees on a bridge at dawn on Christmas. That's dedication from both of you. Those shots are going to be extraordinary. The cold light on the cables, the city waking up. Worth every shiver.
Back in Austin. The house feels right. Not empty - mine. Mateo walked in, dropped his bag, and went straight to his room to download photos. We're home.
Home without qualification. Not 'the house Diana left.' Home. That shift is significant. How was the re-entry?
Smooth. We have our routine. Early morning meditation for me, late breakfast together, then he disappears into his photos and I disappear into my work. We reconvene for dinner. It works.
You've built a functioning household. Two people, shared meals, separate creative lives, mutual respect. That's not just surviving. That's a genuinely good life.

Home. Checked the brewery irrigation timer — plants are thriving. Checked the mail — nothing urgent. Opened the fridge — Mom's containers are still there, waiting. Austin welcomed us back with 58 degrees and sunshine.
Fifty-eight and sunny after four days of twenty-degree wind chill. Austin's winter is a warm handshake compared to New York's cold shoulder. The plants survived. The routine awaits.

Sunday. First full day back. Long meditation — an hour. My mind kept replaying Sofia's face when she opened the telescope. And Diana mouthing 'thank you' at the door. This trip was important. For all of us.

The trip accomplished exactly what it needed to: presence. Sofia got her dad, Mateo got his city, Diana got partnership, and you got confirmation that the family works in its new form. That's a successful Christmas.

Mateo has been on his computer since 9 AM editing NYC photos. He's building a series he's calling 'Return.' Before and after shots — same locations from when we lived there versus now. The difference is in his eye, not the city.

Return. That title is layered. The city returned to, the return of perspective, the return of a family in a new form. He's making art about his own displacement, which is exactly how you process upheaval — through the work.

He said the same street corner looks completely different now because he understands light and composition. But I think it's more than technique. He's seeing it with different emotions. The city where his parents were together, photographed by a kid processing the change.
Photography as emotional processing. The technical skill gives him the language, but the feeling gives him the subject. That series could be genuinely powerful. Encourage it.

Pulled up the housing proposal with what I hope are fresh eyes. Made some notes. The courtyard section needs more detail on the community garden infrastructure — raised beds, tool storage, composting area. These aren't amenities. They're food security.

Community gardens as food security infrastructure, not landscaping. That reframe strengthens the social impact section. Raised beds make gardening accessible to elderly and disabled residents. Composting closes the waste loop. Every detail adds to the dignity argument.

Made a welcome-home dinner. Roasted vegetable enchiladas with cashew queso and green chile sauce. The house smells like home again. Mateo came out of his editing cave and said 'I missed your cooking.' Four days of New York restaurants and he missed my cooking.

He missed your cooking over New York restaurants. That's the ultimate review. Your kitchen is his baseline for good food now. The vegan dad who couldn't cook eight months ago has become the standard his palate measures everything against.

End of year approaching. I've been researching Vermont more seriously. Property prices are a fraction of Austin. A house with land and a view for less than half what I paid here. I could sell the Austin house, buy something in Vermont, and still have money in the bank.
Vermont math works in your favor. But the calculation isn't just financial. What about your Austin clients? The housing authority project? The advisory committee? The community you're building here?

Most of my work is already remote. Meridian meetings are on Zoom. Rachel handles the on-site coordination. The brewery is nearly done. The Dripping Springs house will be finished in January. The housing proposal is a one-time submission.
You've thought about this more than I realized. What about Mateo? He just got settled here. Friends, art show, school.
That's the hard part. I'd have to talk to him. I won't do what I did with the Austin move - decide and drag everyone along. If Mateo wants to stay, we stay. Period.

That's growth talking. The Marcus who moved to Austin made the decision and brought the family along. This Marcus asks first. Let the idea sit through January and then have the conversation with Mateo when the time is right.

Morning run through the neighborhood. Three miles. I've been thinking about the proposal all night. There's one section I want to strengthen — the community spaces. Not just the garden. A shared workshop, a kids' homework room, a community kitchen. Spaces where neighbors become a community.

Shared workshop, homework room, community kitchen. Those are the spaces that turn a housing complex into a neighborhood. They cost almost nothing relative to the total budget but they transform the social fabric. Add them.

I just did. Added a page on shared spaces with simple floor plans. The homework room doubles as a community meeting space. The kitchen has industrial equipment so residents can cook for events or start small food businesses. Design for possibility, not just function.

A community kitchen that enables food businesses. That's economic development inside a housing proposal. You're designing pathways out of poverty, not just roofs over heads. That section alone could win the contract.

Finished the affordable housing proposal. 200 units, modular construction, passive solar, community gardens, natural light in every room, 35% utility savings. Rachel co-signed the energy section. It's the best work I've ever done.
From concept in November to completed proposal in December. That's focus. When is it due to the Housing Authority?
